2025 R+ Classification — Reason-grouped view

2026-06-04 · Single-year (2025) classification · R+ (revenue-up) only · R- excluded from view (data preserved)
Coverage: 98 companies in classification library (IT census target: 122). 2025 records → 82 R+ (revenue-up) · 14 R- (revenue-down, excluded) · 2 unclassified.
R+ split: 41 in R+×S- (revenue up, stock down) — 本命 / primary focus · 41 in R+×S+ (secondary).
On the reason text — what's genuine vs templated:
Each company card now shows three layers. (1) Genuine research (green block, primary) — a source-grounded summary from a per-company JP web search using the prompt 「証券コードが[code]の直近の決算のデータを調べるのとそれが伸びたないしは下がった理由と株価にどういう影響が出たかを教えて」. This is the actual recent-earnings + driver + stock-impact narrative for that specific company, aggregated across the most recent 1-3 years of disclosures. (2) Pattern (small gray collapsed line) — the bucket-templated framing showing which reason group the company landed in. (3) Source prose (click to expand) — the original rawExplanation from the fixture (financial numbers from irbank.net, stock from nikkei.com).
Durably overlooked — multiple business-up years, stock not catching up 3 names
In plain terms: these companies have grown their business steadily for 3 or more years in a row, but the stock price still has not reflected it. Rule used: 2 or more prior years tagged "Q2" (business UP, stock DOWN) — which means at least 3 consecutive years of the same mismatch. Why this matters: if the market overlooks growth once it could be noise — but overlooking it 3 years running suggests something structural. Often these are small or mid caps without good analyst coverage, or names sitting in a sector that institutional investors have rotated away from. If the structural reason resolves (broker coverage starts, sector re-rates, governance improves) the stock can re-rate sharply. This is the strongest "asymmetric upside" signal in the four R+×S- groups.
3697 SHIFT Inc. SaaS mid
Revenue: n/a Stock 2025 YoY: n/a
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 Yahoo finance アナリスト評価記事 2026/6/2 broker downgrade + kabutan 3697 + minkabu 3697
What happened: SHIFT does software quality assurance — they test other companies' software to find bugs before launch. The year ending October 2025 was excellent: sales grew 17% to ¥129.8 billion, profit grew even faster at +48%, and gross margins improved to 34.7%. But the next year started weaker — in the first half of FY8/2026, ordinary profit dropped 16% YoY to ¥6.6 billion. The recent quarterly operating margin also fell from 14.4% to 11.0%. So a record year was followed by a sudden slowdown that the market hadn't fully expected. Why: SHIFT is investing heavily upfront in a new AI-powered service called DQS — hiring more engineers and ramping sales-and-marketing to push it. Hiring that had been restrained in the prior year also normalized this year, so personnel costs jumped. The money goes out as costs today, while the new AI revenue arrives only later — so today's profit is suffering because of tomorrow's investment. SHIFT is in a 'spend first, earn later' valley. Higher short-term borrowing for working capital also bumped interest expense up. Stock reaction: Investors reacted hard. On June 2, 2026 a major Japanese broker downgraded SHIFT from 'Buy' to 'Hold' AND slashed its price target from ¥1,700 to ¥780 — less than half the previous target. Brokers only cut targets that aggressively when they no longer trust when the investment will pay off. The day before, the consensus across 7 analysts had been 'Slightly Bullish' (4.43), which made the sharp reversal even more jarring. The stock had already hit a year-low of ¥590 in February. Why did this hurt so much? SHIFT trades at a PER of about 41 — meaning the stock costs roughly 41 years of current annual profit. The historical range is just 2-15 years. So this is an unusually expensive stock, and expensive stocks get punished hard when profit growth slows. Past M&A still weighs on the books through goodwill amortization, and the company pays no dividend, so long-term holders have no income reason to stick around. What would change the story — (1) DQS needs to win real customers in coming quarters, and (2) spending growth needs to drop below revenue growth so margins can rise again. Until then, the market sees SHIFT as 'a good company buying expensive growth too aggressively.'
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue compounded each year for the past 3 years on durable business execution, BUT the stock has failed to re-rate (2025 stock flat/— YoY) — the gap is that the market's residual derating stance from prior years hasn't broken despite multi-year consistent growth. Durability of the mismatch is what makes it worth attention.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (strategic-investment margin trough):* the inverse split-signal year — revenue still growing but profit DOWN, because SHIFT front-loaded heavy AI investment (building the DQS modernization service), spiking SG&A. Q1 FY8/2026 operating profit fell 19.9% to ¥2.8bn, missing expectations. The stock cratered from the ~¥1,200 area (Oct 2025) toward ~¥666 (Apr 2026), roughly -49% over six months and ~-64% off the trailing-year high of ¥1,827.5. Revenue ◎ / profit ✕ (deliberate investment, not demand loss), stock ✕✕.
4071 Plus Alpha Consulting Co.,LTD. SaaS small
Revenue: n/a Stock 2025 YoY: n/a
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4071 + minkabu 4071 (4071の理論株価) + Yahoo finance 4071 + IR resources
What happened: Plus Alpha Consulting makes HR-tech software for big companies — basically a cloud platform that helps with employee evaluations and talent management. The latest results (announced May 13, 2026) were clean: sales grew 14% to ¥9.34 billion, and profit grew much faster — up 32% to ¥3.69 billion. Over 5 years, revenue is up 3.6x and net profit is up 3.2x. Margins are improving every quarter. The balance sheet is rock solid too, with an 81% equity ratio (meaning very little debt). Why: The flagship HR Solutions business keeps winning enterprise customers — the main growth engine. At the same time, the company got smarter about how it spends marketing money, so profit grew faster than revenue. Both the product and the cost discipline are working — a clean print. Stock reaction: The stock closed at ¥2,335 on May 11, 2026 — barely moving despite the strong results. This is the central puzzle for this name. Why isn't the market reacting? A few practical reasons: First, Plus Alpha listed in 2021, right at the top of the growth-stock boom. The stock has been normalizing down from that initial premium ever since, dragged down by the broader cooling of growth-stock valuations. Second, the company is mid-sized — its market value sits in the low hundreds of billions of yen, which is below the minimum size many big institutional investors (pension funds, mutual funds) require. So those buyers don't show up. Third, big banks don't write much research about it, so good news doesn't get amplified into headlines. The silver lining: Plus Alpha's current PER (price-to-earnings ratio — meaning how many years of current profit the stock costs) is 19.6x. Its historical PER range has been 20-100x, so right now it's at the BOTTOM of its own valuation history. There's plenty of room to re-rate upward if a catalyst arrives. The catalysts to watch — a big customer-win announcement, a major bank starting to cover the stock, or just a few more quarters of these compounding numbers. Until then, it's the classic 'quietly compounding while the market sleeps' setup.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue compounded each year for the past 4 years on durable business execution, BUT the stock has failed to re-rate (2025 stock flat/— YoY) — the gap is that the market's residual derating stance from prior years hasn't broken despite multi-year consistent growth. Durability of the mismatch is what makes it worth attention.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (record results — derating finally arrests):* stock ¥1,163 (Apr 7) – ¥2,612 (Aug 14); Apr 2025 ¥1,163 was the absolute multi-year low (-74% from peak), then recovered to ¥2,612 by August (+125% off the spring low). **FY9/2025 actual (announced Nov 14, 2025): revenue ¥17,084M (+22.8% YoY organic / +83% reported including segment expansion), operating profit ¥6,378M (+40.8% YoY), operating margin 37.3%** (high for SaaS); enterprise customer ratio rising, ARPU rising, HR Solutions segment leading. Plan achievement: revenue 100.5%, OP 104.6% per IR. Sign summary: revenue rose +22.8% YoY organically; operating profit rose +40.8%; operating margin 37.3% record; shares rallied off ¥1,163 spring low to ¥2,612 high (Aug 14) — +125% intra-year. 業績 ◎◎ (record results + margin expansion) / 株価 ◎ (recovery from multi-year low).

Cautious side: stock still well below the Nov 2021 peak of ¥4,480 (Aug 2025 high ¥2,612 = -42% off ATH); the 2021 peak was hype-driven post-IPO mania at a level unjustified by fundamentals, so it may not be a meaningful target; revenue mix shift from SMB toward enterprise raises ARPU but lengthens sales cycle; competitive HR-SaaS pressure (Money Forward, freee, Visional all expanding).

Sources:
- https://equity.jiji.com/storage/tdnet/140120241107515258.pdf (FY9/2024 通期決算短信, Nov 8, 2024)
- https://finance-frontend-pc-dist.west.edge.storage-yahoo.jp/disclosure/20251114/20251113501423.pdf (FY9/2025 通期決算短信, Nov 14, 2025)
- https://finance.logmi.jp/articles/378612 (FY23 +41.2% / +39.3% strong-growth context + mid-term plan revision)
- https://finance.logmi.jp/articles/382931 (FY25 record + FY26 plan + enterprise-ratio rise)
- https://finance-frontend-pc-dist.west.edge.storage-yahoo.jp/disclosure/20251114/20251113500859.pdf (FY9/2025 4Q 決算説明資料)
- https://www.nikkei.com/nkd/company/history/yprice/?scode=4071 (Nikkei 10-year price history)
4165 PLAID,Inc. SaaS small
Revenue: n/a Stock 2025 YoY: n/a
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4165 + minkabu 4165 + note.com HR793/yuthinn PLAID analysis + invest leaders article on rate hikes & growth stocks
What happened: PLAID makes a software product called KARTE — it's a customer experience (CX) platform that collects behavior data on people visiting a company's website and decides in real time 'what should we show this person next.' First half FY12/2026: revenue grew 18.6% to ¥7.77 billion, but profit fell YoY. Full-year guidance is aggressive: revenue ¥20.58bn (+53.6%), operating profit ¥2.18bn (+52.5%). Q1 had revenue up 15.9% but operating profit was DOWN -30.1% — costs are running ahead of revenue. Why: PLAID is investing heavily upfront in sales staff and marketing to push KARTE into bigger enterprise customers. The investment hits costs first; matching revenue arrives later — classic SaaS 'invest now, harvest later' pattern. Stock reaction: PLAID's stock has been stuck for years, and several structural forces are pressing it down at once. The biggest one is interest rates. When rates go up, growth stocks like PLAID cool off — investors prefer cheaper, profit-rich 'value' stocks instead. Data from 1983-2015 shows that during rising-rate periods, value stocks returned +14.4%/year vs growth stocks at +8.3%/year. PLAID is caught in that headwind. The competitive landscape is also tough. The Round 2 web search confirmed PLAID's real competitors: Repro and b→dash in Japan, plus Salesforce, Adobe, and Braze from overseas. PLAID is actually #2 in Japan's CX-platform market behind Salesforce, but competing with global giants is a never-ending grind. On top of that, no major brokerage covers PLAID ('Analyst-coverage: none'). That means institutional investors don't get research notes on it, so the big buyers stay away. The dividend is also zero, so income-focused investors won't touch it. And there's product-concentration risk — KARTE is essentially the entire company, so investors stay cautious until a 'second leg' shows up. The trigger for a re-rating: PLAID needs to deliver a quarter where KARTE's revenue jumps AND margins expand at the same time. That would prove the front-loaded investment is actually converting to profit, and the market view would shift.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue compounded each year for the past 4 years on durable business execution, BUT the stock has failed to re-rate (2025 stock flat/— YoY) — the gap is that the market's residual derating stance from prior years hasn't broken despite multi-year consistent growth. Durability of the mismatch is what makes it worth attention.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (FY9/2025 — growth holds, profit thin):* for the fiscal year ended September 30, 2025, revenue was ¥13.40bn, up 21.87% — strong top line. But profitability is thin and wobbling: net margin only ~7%, EBITDA margin ~3%, the most recent quarter's revenue (¥3.29bn) slightly missed the ¥3.35bn estimate, and quarterly net income fell ~32% sequentially. 業績 ◎ on revenue, but with a profit-quality caveat; 株価 ✕ (still weak) → Q2, medium confidence — watch the profit line, because the ◎ is being carried by revenue while earnings wobble.
All financials up, stock still fell — clean mismatch or profit-taking sell-off 2 names
In plain terms: revenue grew, operating profit grew, AND net profit grew — but the stock STILL fell 30% or more in the year. Rule used: rev_dir=up, op_dir=up, net_dir=up, AND stock YoY < -30%. Why this matters: there is no operational excuse — the gap between fundamentals and price is unusually clean. The most common explanations are (a) profit-taking after a prior rally that took the stock too far ahead of earnings, (b) a one-off event scare (regulation rumor, key-person resignation, accounting noise) that did NOT actually affect the business, or (c) sector rotation pulling capital out indiscriminately. These can be the cleanest mean-reversion opportunities — IF you can confirm the scare or rotation is the real cause and the underlying business is still on track.
5244 jig.jp Co., Ltd. SI_IT_services small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -34.0%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 5244 + minkabu 5244 (売り予想数 ranking 5/20/2026) + Yahoo finance 5244
What happened: jig.jp's main business is the live-streaming app 'FUWATCH' — a C2C live-streaming platform where streamers broadcast video or live to viewers, and viewers send gifts to streamers (which becomes the streamer's income). FY3/2026: revenue +6.1% to ¥14.6 billion (slight up), operating profit -1.8% to ¥1.98 billion (slight down). Net margin and ROE (return on equity) compressed YoY; equity ratio rose. Why: FUWATCH performed well and held up the top line. But three things simultaneously compressed profit: personnel cost increases, expanded ad spend to acquire new users, and investment in new businesses. Stock reaction: On the announcement day, the stock fell only -0.71% to ¥278 — but for full-year 2025, it's down -34% YoY, a significant medium-term decline. On top of that, the FY3/2027 guidance (announced May 14, 2026) projects OP of just ¥1.98 billion (+0.2%) — essentially flat. Management's reasoning: 'rising personnel costs, expanded user-acquisition ad spend and event costs, and new-business investment will weigh on profit.' The market doesn't like this. Several structural factors are at play. First, live-streaming market competition intensifying. Japan's live-streaming market is seeing well-capitalized new entrants, and the fight for streamer talent is intensifying. The risk of rising CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost — what you spend to win each new user) is surfacing, and that creates structural margin pressure for jig.jp. Second, market distaste for margin compression. When operating-profit growth stalls and margin contracts, the market easily reads it as 'the end of the growth narrative.' Third, the small-cap problem. jig.jp is a TSE Growth small-cap with limited institutional coverage — structural absence of buyers. Fourth, weak retail sentiment. On May 20, 2026, jig.jp appeared #4 on minkabu's 'Rising Sell-Recommendation Count' ranking — meaning retail investors are increasingly forecasting it will fall. jig.jp's balance sheet (equity ratio 66.9%) is strong, so there's no insolvency risk. But the 'growth-stock appeal' is fading, and the stock is likely to stay in a 'stable but not rising' state. Re-rating requires (a) a hit new business beyond FUWATCH, or (b) building a new growth axis through overseas expansion.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue, operating profit, and net profit ALL grew sharply in 2025 (clean three-line acceleration), BUT the stock fell -34.0% YoY — the gap is a clean directional disconnect at material size. Either the market is pricing in a non-financial concern (governance, accounting, sector risk) the headline numbers don't show, or the market simply hasn't caught up yet.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥201 (4-7) – ¥339 (1-15); close ¥216 (-34.0% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥13800M (+12.7%), OP ¥1500M (+36.4%), NP ¥1050M (+23.5%). 業績 ◎ / 株価 ✕.

3778 SAKURA internet Inc. SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -37.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3778 + sakura.ad.jp 2026 決算説明資料 + Nikkei article + 三菱UFJ信託 データセンター投資情報
What happened: SAKURA internet is a Japanese data-center and cloud-services provider — they sell servers, cloud, and more recently GPU infrastructure for generative AI to corporate customers. FY3/2026 revenue grew +12.4% to ¥35.3 billion, but profits essentially collapsed. The prior year had an operating profit of ¥4.15 billion; this year it flipped to an operating loss of ¥0.40 billion. Ordinary profit fell -97.4% to ¥105 million, and net profit fell -92.6% to ¥216 million. By segment: GPU infrastructure grew +20.3% and cloud grew +9.4%, but the legacy physical-infrastructure business shrank -7.2%. Why: Heavy investment in generative-AI infrastructure blew up the profit line — bulk GPU server purchases, mass engineer hiring, data-center lease expansion, and surging depreciation (accounting cost for capital investment spread over time) all hit at once. AI-related segments grew, but the contraction in the older physical-infrastructure business partly offset it. Stock reaction: When earnings were announced on April 27, the stock dropped -9.60% to ¥3,295 on volume up +415%. It kept falling — ¥3,100 → ¥2,939 between May 28 and June 3. A major Japanese broker held its rating at 'Neutral' on April 28 but cut its price target to ¥3,093. The market's logic is clear — SAKURA was a hot 'Japanese AI-cloud play' during 2023-2024, with the stock pushed to premium valuation on the GPU-infrastructure story. That story is now cooling. Generative-AI capex growth no longer has the past momentum, and SAKURA's own profit collapse confirms that the investment phase is real and expensive. Structural overhangs are heavy. First, competition — AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud (the hyperscalers) operate at vastly larger scale; SAKURA is a small player by comparison. And in Japan specifically, there's intensifying competition in the narrow 'government cloud' procurement market. The investor mix is also a problem. SAKURA is a small-cap currently in losses, so institutional coverage is thin. Data-center investment dollars are flowing to AI-chip giants like NVIDIA, not to Japanese mid-caps like SAKURA. On top of that, rising electricity prices, construction-material costs, and labor costs all create structural cost pressure. The FY3/2027 guide projects recovery (revenue ¥45.0 billion +27.5%, OP back to ¥1.5 billion), but the market wants proof that the new GPU capacity actually fills with paying AI demand. Until that's visible, getting back to the 2024 highs will be difficult.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: FY3/2025 fundamentals grew sharply (rev +44%, OP +369%, NP +351%) on Sakura Cloud / generative-AI demand — BUT the stock fell -37.5% YoY because the generative-AI-cloud narrative cooled off the 2023-2024 mania peak. The gap is sector-narrative cooling, not a fresh business mismatch the analyst hasn't seen.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥2677 (7-30) – ¥5020 (2-4); close ¥2780 (-37.5% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥31412M (+43.9%), OP ¥4145M (+368.7%), NP ¥2937M (+350.7%). 業績 ◎ / 株価 ✕.

Revenue up, profit compressed — derated on margins, potentially recoverable 17 names
In plain terms: revenue keeps growing, but operating margins have been squeezed. Rule used: 業績 sign = "mixed" (= revenue up, but operating profit OR net profit down). Why this matters: the squeeze is usually one of four things — (a) up-front investment (R&D, marketing, hiring) that depresses current profit but builds future revenue, (b) input-cost inflation (wages, materials, energy), (c) pricing pressure from competition, or (d) FX / one-off impairments. The market has marked the multiple DOWN because earnings look weaker, but the business still has top-line momentum. What to watch: the margin trend in the next 2-3 quarters. If management has a credible cost or pricing plan, margin recovery often triggers a sharp re-rating because both EPS and the multiple rise together. This is the "patient money" cohort — recovery typically takes 1-2 years.
3653 Morpho Inc. other small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -41.6%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3653 + minkabu 3653 + kabuline 3653 forum
What happened: Morpho develops and licenses image-processing AI software technology — its tech is embedded in smartphone camera face-recognition and image-correction algorithms, automotive image recognition, industrial machinery vision, and many other devices where customers don't see Morpho's name. FY10/2026 Q1 was rough — revenue fell -32.2% to ¥470 million, with a ¥390 million operating loss — sharp deterioration. The past 12 quarters (3 years) show an ongoing worsening trend, with both operating and net margins deeply negative throughout. Why: The direct cause is weakness in the smart-device segment (the main business). On top of that, two longer-term headwinds: the smartphone market itself is maturing (no more growth), and AI image processing is becoming open-source and commoditized (a normal technology anyone can use). The 'specialty image-processing technology' that used to be Morpho's moat is gradually eroding. Stock reaction: The stock is weak — the market doesn't believe management's full-year 'revenue and profit recovery' guide. Honestly, Morpho doesn't quite fit the typical R+×S- ('business up / stock down') pattern. The reality is closer to 'the business itself is structurally deteriorating.' The stock isn't stuck because the market is being unfair — it's stuck because the market's cold assessment is probably correct. Management is betting on an H2 rebound, but the -32% Q1 drop is too large to be made up in H2. Even if H2 is perfect, the full-year math is hard. Morpho's balance sheet (equity ratio) is high, which prevents a deeper crash — meaning there's no insolvency risk. But its appeal as a growth name has clearly faded. Re-rating triggers — (a) the smart-device business stops declining and stabilizes, (b) new AI image-processing licensing wins prove differentiation, or (c) multi-year contracts with major automotive or industrial customers get announced. Until then, low-volume wait-and-see drift continues. AI-theme rallies might lift the stock temporarily, but without fundamentals backing them up, those rallies don't last.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -41.6% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥775 (Dec 23) – ¥1,536 (Feb 14); close ¥790 (-41.6% YoY). FY10/2025 rev ¥3.36B (+2% flat-up), OP ¥45M (-82%), NP -¥77M (back to loss). 業績 ✕ (OP collapsed -82%, NP back to loss; turn proved fragile) / 株価 ✕.

<<CANONICAL FY2025>>

3668 COLOPL,Inc. content_games small
Revenue: mixed Stock 2025 YoY: -11.1%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 Nikkei article on FY9/2024 first-ever loss + gamebiz 3668 + Time Bank Securities article on 7 reasons + 投資の達人 article + note 運営型モバイルゲーム article
What happened: COLOPL is a Japanese smartphone game developer and operator — historically known for hits like 'Shironeko Project,' 'Quiz RPG: Wizard and Black Cat Wiz,' and 'Dragon Quest Walk.' Mid-year results announced May 7, 2026 were rough: revenue -28.2% to ¥10.1 billion, operating profit -62.3% to ¥533 million. FY9/2024 was the first-ever full-year loss since listing (revenue ¥25.97 billion, operating loss ¥1.21 billion, net loss ¥1.87 billion). Why: The main mobile game 'Shironeko Project' has lost the brightness of its hit era — while it can climb the App Store rankings during collaboration events, the gradual revenue decline typical of titles past their 10-year mark continues. Other legacy titles are also gradually declining. This reflects 'the structural disadvantage of operated mobile games' — platform fees on the App Store and Google Play (30% of revenue) and user-acquisition ad costs are flat regardless of company size, so smaller and mid-sized publishers fight an uphill battle against bigger ones. Stock reaction: The day after the mid-year print (May 8), the stock fell -7.64% (-¥32). Multiple structural problems are in the backdrop. First, mobile-game sector-wide derating. Japan's smartphone-game market has been roughly flat for years now. Chinese games (Genshin Impact, Azur Lane, etc.) are now normal in Japan, which means Japanese companies are watching their share of a not-growing pie shrink structurally. Second, gacha regulatory risk. Psychologically, gacha mechanics are classified as 'variable-ratio reinforcement schedules,' which the literature considers gambling-addiction-inducing. Over 50% of revenue comes from just the top 1-2% of users ('whales'), so the business model rests on thin ice. There's a history of regulatory tightening (banning comp-gacha, requiring probability disclosure), and more regulation is always a possibility hanging overhead. Third, analyst coverage. A statistic from the Nikkei: 70.5% of Japanese listed companies (about 2,870 names) have NO analyst price-target coverage. COLOPL is likely in that 'zero coverage' bucket. Institutional investors typically can't deploy capital meaningfully into names that aren't covered by analysts, so there are simply no buyers showing up. Fourth, PBR (price-to-book ratio) at 0.93x — meaning the stock trades below the company's net asset value (i.e., breakup value). That's the market saying 'we view the future of this business negatively.' But there's a counter-narrative. Looking longer term, COLOPL's net margins have been improving, interest-bearing debt has been shrinking, and a quiet restructuring is underway. Cost cuts are working. The market is locked into the 'legacy mobile decline' narrative and isn't pricing in this quiet improvement. Re-rating triggers — (a) a new mobile-game title showing hit potential, (b) a new VR/metaverse release, or (c) the investment-incubation business stopping its drag on profit.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -11.1% YoY because operating margins compressed (NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥407 (4-7) – ¥545 (11-12); close ¥429 (-11.1% YoY). FY9/2025 rev ¥25933M (-0.2%), OP ¥1002M, NP ¥-306M (loss). 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

<<CANONICAL FY2025>>

3760 CAVE Interactive CO.,LTD. content_games small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -29.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3760 + Yahoo finance 3760 + scouter monex 3760
What happened: CAVE Interactive is a Japanese game developer specializing in shoot-em-ups — known for arcade-era classic IPs like 'Dodonpachi,' 'Mushihimesama,' and 'Deathsmiles.' Today the company runs mobile-game adaptations of those IPs and a video-distribution-related business. The most recent Q1 alone looks fine: ordinary profit ¥365 million, Q1 operating profit +92.1%. But the longer picture is different — average revenue growth over the past 3 years was +114.8% (very high), but this year's guide is for revenue to swing to -14.8% (a decline). The trend is flipping sharply. Why: The growth engine in the flagship shoot-em-up IP mobile portfolio has stalled. The video-distribution-related business is growing fast (+325.6%), but off a tiny base, so it can't compensate for the game-side decline. CAVE's arcade-era IPs are reaching the limits of their mobile-extension life cycle. Stock reaction: The stock is in a downtrend; 1-year total return is -29.21%, so it's been weak for a while. Honestly, CAVE doesn't fit the classic R+×S- ('good business / bad stock') pattern. The reality is closer to 'the business itself is hitting an inflection point, and the market is pricing in the end of the high-growth narrative.' The cold market reception is harsh but rational given where the fundamentals are headed. Structural difficulties — first, the broad mobile-game industry is saturated and competitive, with overseas players invading. Second, the shoot-em-up niche makes 'hit-or-miss' new-title risk especially acute — big hits are hard to engineer in that genre. Third, as a TSE Standard small-cap, CAVE has limited institutional analyst coverage. Even when profitable, the gap between actual results and 'market growth expectations' keeps widening. CAVE's classic arcade IPs have largely been monetized on mobile already (the well is running dry), and the absence of new flagship hits suppresses long-term valuation. The stock's PBR (price-to-book ratio) of 1.98x is below peers, meaning the deterioration is already priced in. The market has effectively decided 'this name's high-growth story is over.' With expectations bombed-out, even small positive news (a hit new title, an IP licensing deal) could move the stock meaningfully — but until then, low-volume drift in the depressed range continues.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -29.4% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥681 (4-7) – ¥1100 (11-12); close ¥734 (-29.4% YoY). FY5/2025 rev ¥13969M (+13.8%), OP ¥113M (-39.5%), NP ¥24M (-83.7%). 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

3776 BroadBand Tower, Inc. SI_IT_services small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -0.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3776 + nikkei 3776 + irbank 3776
What happened: BroadBand Tower (BBT) is a Japanese data-center operator running two main businesses: physical data-center services (hosting corporate servers) and corporate data-solution services. FY2026 Q1 was good: revenue +2.9% to ¥3.19 billion, ordinary profit +63.8% to ¥226 million. Earlier, in October, management had also raised the FY2025 ordinary-profit guide significantly (¥0.64bn → ¥0.85bn, +32.8%), narrowing the implied profit decline from -28% to -4.4%. BUT the FY12/2026 outlook is harsh again: revenue -12.4%, OP -38.4%, ordinary profit -46.1%. Why: Both the data-center business and the data-solution business are forecast to decline next year. The data-center business should structurally be a growth driver — but companies are increasingly migrating workloads from on-prem (their own servers) to hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), and that trend is likely pressuring BBT's own data-center utilization. Stock reaction: The stock peaked recently at ¥276 (May 22), dropped -5.95% on May 26, and is ranging ¥255-266 with no clear direction. The market's read is clear — 'a brief recovery, but the next-year guide is so bad it'll fall again.' The FY2025 upward revision was good news, but with FY2026 guiding to -46% in ordinary profit, the market concluded 'this isn't a structural turn — 2025 was just a lucky year.' Several structural problems on top. First, valuation is extreme — BroadBand Tower's PER (price-to-earnings ratio — how many years of current profit the stock costs) is about 157x. Historically, between 2010 and 2025, its PER ranged from 'negative (losses)' to a maximum of 757x — an extremely wide range. That's the profile of 'a speculative-moving stock,' and institutional investors avoid that volatility. Second, scale. BBT's market cap is about ¥15.8 billion — well below the minimum-size threshold that most big institutional investors require. Even if they wanted to buy, they couldn't build a meaningful position with the available liquidity. Third, market interpretation. Temporary improvements are read as 'not structural turning points.' The FY2025 upward revision wasn't taken as 'a great surprise' but as 'a one-off lucky stretch.' Fourth, financial-statement concerns — equity-ratio issues and other balance-sheet worries make institutions cautious. What would unlock a re-rating — (a) announcing new data-center contract wins, (b) showing a clear strategic pivot (e.g., AI-demand GPU clusters), and (c) issuing transparent segment-level guidance. Until then, low-volume range-bound trading continues.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -0.5% YoY because operating margins compressed (NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥116 (6-19) – ¥206 (1-10); close ¥183 (-0.5% YoY). FY12/2025 rev ¥15289M (+13.9%), OP ¥811M (+21.4%), NP ¥300M (-25.8%). 業績 unclassified / 株価 unclassified.

3844 COMTURE CORPORATION SI_IT_services small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -24.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3844 + Yahoo finance 3844 forum + ses-beginner article on independent SIer challenges + bitget wiki 3844
What happened: COMTURE is an independent Japanese SIer (Systems Integrator) — a company that develops, maintains, and operates IT systems for large and mid-sized corporate clients. FY3/2026 Q3 revenue grew +4.4% to ¥28.0 billion, but operating profit was nearly flat at +0.7%. Gross profit (revenue minus cost of goods) grew +4.9%, but the OP line below that barely moved. Next year (FY3/2027) guidance: revenue +10.2% to ¥42.0 billion, OP +0.8% to ¥4.7 billion. Even with that modest OP growth, it would be 14 consecutive years of record profit — a rare achievement among Japanese listed companies. Why: Multiple cost pressures hit OP at once: (1) wage hikes and headcount growth lifted labor costs; (2) new-graduate training costs; (3) office-consolidation costs for group business integration; (4) company-wide event costs for engagement; (5) new core-system rollout costs. All of these are 'reinvestment for long-term competitiveness' — sacrificing short-term margin. Stock reaction: Even though results stay strong and the dividend keeps rising, the stock is uninspiring. Why? Investor sentiment shows up clearly on message boards. Quotes seen: 'No future, just a plain stock with low attention' and 'No in-house products, growing engineers but not particularly strong sales — just another independent SIer.' That captures the actual valuation cap. Structural reasons — First, the fundamental challenge of independent SIers. The expectation is that 'SIers without unique strengths or in-house products (pure contract-development shops) can't survive long term.' Non-IT companies are increasingly hiring their own engineers, and pure-contract SIers are being squeezed out. COMTURE's in-house products are weak, putting it in this vulnerable category. Second, lack of thematic relevance. The flashy growth themes — AI, data centers, quantum computing — pass COMTURE by, since its main business is plain contract development. Media doesn't cover it, and institutional growth investors don't talk about it. Third, IT/SIer-industry bifurcation. Within the industry, high-growth zones (SaaS, AI-related, specialized services) and stagnant zones (traditional SI) are increasingly separated. COMTURE tends to get bucketed in the latter. Fourth, wage inflation as a structural margin compressor. Japan's talent-acquisition competition keeps intensifying, and wages keep rising. For a labor-intensive business like COMTURE, that's a persistent headwind. Despite 14 consecutive years of record profit, the market just can't find a 'growth narrative' in COMTURE. Re-rating triggers — (a) the digitalization-investment cycle turning into actual profit (possible FY3/2027 or later), (b) a major dividend hike or buyback that puts it in income-investor portfolios, or (c) a sector rotation into IT-infrastructure names.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -24.4% YoY because operating margins compressed (profit lines under pressure). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥1373 (4-7) – ¥2269 (7-23); close ¥1710 (-24.4% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥36341M (+6.3%), OP ¥4630M (+0.7%), NP ¥3160M (+0.8%). 業績 unclassified / 株価 ✕.

3858 Ubiquitous AI Corporation SI_IT_services small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -14.2%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3858 + Nikkei article on 70.5% no-coverage stat + Toyo Keizai article on institutional small-cap dynamics + manabow Q&A on mid/small cap research
What happened: Ubiquitous AI (formerly 'Ubiquitous') makes embedded-software middleware — middleware is the layer of software that sits inside IoT devices, cars, home appliances, and other devices. The company is strategically pivoting toward an AI focus, signaled by the name change. FY3/2026 results were tough: revenue -5.2% to ¥3.92 billion, operating loss ¥201 million. On top of that, the company booked a goodwill impairment loss and continued restructuring costs, pushing net loss to ¥518 million. Next year is also expected to be loss-making, and the past 12 quarters show a worsening trend. Why: 'Goodwill' is an intangible asset recorded when a company buys another for more than its net asset value. When the acquired business underperforms, goodwill is written down as 'unrecoverable' (impairment). That happened here. The company is also restructuring (cleaning up unprofitable businesses and adjusting headcount), and that creates near-term costs. The name change from 'Ubiquitous' to 'Ubiquitous AI' signals the strategic AI pivot, but actual AI-business monetization is still ahead. Stock reaction: Surprisingly, the stock is fairly stable — ¥349, +1.76% past month, +3.87% past year. It hit a high of ¥615 in January 2025, then drifted down to the mid-¥400s and now trades below ¥400. No big move in either direction overall — a strange kind of stability for a loss-making name. Why is it so stable despite the losses, and why is upside limited? Several structural reasons combine. First, Japan's market-wide analyst-coverage gap. This affects most Japanese small-caps. As of Dec 2024, 2,870 Japanese listed companies (70.5% of all listed companies) had no analyst price-target coverage. The top 50 names by market cap get an average of 10.7 analysts each, but research concentrates on large-caps — small-caps like Ubiquitous AI fall outside the lens. Second, institutional small-cap avoidance. Pension funds and mutual funds running huge portfolios structurally can't take meaningful positions in tiny companies. There's also liquidity risk (the risk you can't trade when you want to). Third, sell-side research economics. Covering names institutions don't follow doesn't generate brokerage commissions, so the cost-benefit math of research doesn't work out — coverage naturally falls off. Fourth, the volatility of small-caps. Thin coverage leads to thin trading, and thin trading leads to high volatility — which makes institutions avoid even more (a vicious cycle). There's actually an unusual upside to Ubiquitous AI's thin trading — sellers are also scarce, so the stock doesn't fall hard even when losses are reported. Re-rating triggers: (a) winning a major Edge AI (AI that runs on local devices) contract, (b) a clean profitable print once restructuring completes, or (c) parent-company ownership increase or an MBO (management buy-out).
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -14.2% YoY because operating margins compressed (profit lines under pressure). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥277 (4-7) – ¥615 (11-12); close ¥363 (-14.2% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥4138M (+19.0%), OP ¥105M (+47.9%), NP ¥97M. 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

3914 JIG-SAW株式会社 other small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -20.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3914 + minkabu 3914 + irbank 3914 + IFIS 株予報 3914
What happened: JIG-SAW provides 24/7 monitoring and operations services for cloud infrastructure — when companies use cloud services like AWS or Azure, JIG-SAW works behind the scenes to monitor system uptime and handle issues. The company also offers IoT (Internet of Things — connecting sensors and devices to the internet) services. FY12/2026 Q1 results were record-breaking: revenue +17.4% to ¥1.04 billion (first quarter ever crossing ¥1bn), operating profit +64.2% to ¥240 million, ordinary profit +60.8% to ¥242 million. The prior period (Q3 cumulative FY12/2024) had been OP -4.6% on front-loaded investment, so Q1's sharp rebound is particularly striking. Why: The flagship system-management business is performing well, and new IoT-related services are rolling out. The prior period's upfront investments are now entering a monetization phase. Stock reaction: Despite the strong Q1, the stock is down -37.71% over the past 12 months — Q1's print didn't fully reverse the longer downtrend. Several structural factors are capping it. First, valuation. JIG-SAW's PER (price-to-earnings ratio — how many years of current profit the stock costs) is 45.59x, and PBR (price-to-book ratio) is 5.56x. Both are 'relatively high,' so market expectations are already priced in. Stocks like this get punished quickly when results miss even slightly. Second, the small-cap growth stock problem. JIG-SAW is a small-cap on TSE Growth, with limited major-broker analyst coverage. As mentioned in other entries, 70.5% of Japanese listed companies have no analyst coverage, and JIG-SAW faces this structural problem. Third, the lack of disclosed guidance. Management cites global uncertainties and the difficulty of balancing expansion and investment, and refuses to publish full-year guidance numbers. Investors get uneasy when 'the future is invisible.' Fourth, competitive concerns. The IoT and software space has many competitors, so it's hard to peer-compare JIG-SAW against similar companies (unclear what the benchmark even is). That makes valuation harder. Fifth, Growth market dynamics. TSE Growth is dominated by retail investors, with limited institutional positioning (especially from foreign investors). Without big buyers, even great prints don't move the stock much. Re-rating triggers — (a) consecutive Q2/Q3 beats, (b) a multi-year contract with a major enterprise customer, or (c) a clear IoT/AI differentiation story.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -20.4% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥2,208 (Oct 17) – ¥4,290 (Oct 31); close ¥2,690 (-20.4% YoY). FY12/2025 rev ¥3.63B (+4%), OP ¥549M (-1%), NP ¥418M (-13%). 業績 ✕ (revenue growth decelerating + NP -13% material; margin compression continuing from 2024) / 株価 ✕.

3983 oRo Co., Ltd. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -18.1%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3983 + minkabu 3983 (アナリスト対象外 confirmed)
What happened: oRo runs two businesses — Cloud Solutions, where they provide 'ZAC' series enterprise core systems (especially project-management and accounting integration), and Marketing Solutions, where they offer digital-marketing support services for companies. FY12/2026 Q1 was good: revenue +18.7% to ¥2.35 billion, operating profit +22.3% to ¥812 million. Both businesses are growing. Why: ZAC series (mainly enterprise systems for project-based businesses like IT, consultancies, and ad agencies) is growing steadily. Marketing Solutions profit also grew sharply — that side led the profit growth. But net margin compressed YoY, signaling a slight weakening of profitability momentum. Stock reaction: Strong results, but the stock is weak — past week -6.08%, past month -1.30%, past year -29.32% — a clear medium-term downtrend. Several structural reasons hold it down. First, the direct problem: 'analyst coverage: none.' The Round 2 web search confirmed this explicitly — no major-brokerage analysts cover oRo. Institutional investors typically can't deploy big money into stocks with no analyst rating. So the buyers simply aren't there. Second, the meaning of the low valuation. oRo's forward PER (price-to-earnings ratio — how many years of current profit the stock costs) is about 15x — relatively low. That means 'the market is not giving oRo a growth-stock premium.' Usually SaaS and growth names trade above 20x PER, but oRo's valuation is in line with a normal mid-tier company. Third, how investors interpret the net-margin compression. When margins fall, the market tends to read it as 'growth is maturing, operating leverage (the dynamic where profit grows faster than revenue at scale) isn't kicking in.' That undermines the long-run upside thesis. Fourth, the awkward portfolio. oRo operates two businesses, but each one alone has many mid-sized competitors. It doesn't get the pure-play SaaS premium that a Cloud-Solutions-only company would get, and it doesn't get the pure-play marketing premium either. 'Chasing two rabbits' dilutes the valuation. To break the downtrend, oRo needs (a) margin expansion to resume (proving operating leverage), (b) M&A interest from a major SaaS player, or (c) measurable two-business synergy that they can put numbers behind.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -18.1% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)

4053 Sun* Inc. SI_IT_services small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -35.7%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 Yahoo finance 4053 + minkabu 4053 (アナリスト対象外) + kabuyoho 4053
What happened: Sun Asterisk (often shortened to 'Sun*') is a company that does 'digital product co-creation' — they accompany corporate clients through launching new business ventures. They use significant offshore development (outsourcing development to overseas teams), with major bases in Vietnam where they employ many engineers. Recent Q1 looked good: revenue +24.6% to ¥4.41 billion, operating profit +113.2% to ¥622 million — sharp rebound. The main Creative & Engineering business hit ¥3.51 billion (+26.3%) and drove the result. BUT looking across the past 12 quarters (3 years), the underlying trend is worsening: net margin and ROE (return on equity) compressed YoY, and the equity ratio is drifting down. Why: Q1's rebound is partly just bouncing off a weak prior-year base. The full-year ordinary-profit growth guide is -25.4%, so forward profit-growth concerns persist. The shock of the FY2025 downward revision is still preventing market trust from recovering. Stock reaction: Results are improving, but the stock doesn't get credit. The first problem: on minkabu, Sun* is explicitly labeled 'Analyst-coverage: none.' That means zero institutional coverage, no research reports, no price targets. This structurally blocks institutional investors from building positions. Second, operating profit is growing but net margin and ROE are falling — a 'quality of earnings' problem. Investors start asking 'does this profit look real, but is it sustainable?' Third, the market-cap problem. Sun*'s market cap is about ¥18.2 billion — mid-cap level that misses the minimum-size threshold for many big institutions. Even if they wanted to position, the scale is awkward. Fourth, sector competition. The Information & Communications sector has fierce competition; single good quarters don't translate to long-term re-rating. Without a clear differentiation story, the market won't bid up the multiple. Most important: Sun*'s original investment thesis (the pitch to investors) was broken once. The original story was 'capacity-led growth + operating leverage' — meaning, hire more engineers, revenue grows, AND margins expand alongside (a SaaS-like growth story). But when customer growth slowed below expectations and reliance on external partners (which carry lower gross margin than in-house engineers) created drag, that thesis broke. Investor trust is slow to rebuild once it's lost. Until margins re-expand (after rebalancing the engineer-vs-partner mix), the stock will stay heavy. Even small future beats probably won't restore the prior growth-stock multiple.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -35.7% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)

4344 Sourcenext Corporation SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -12.6%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 Nikkei article on FY3/2025 ¥38bn loss + minkabu 4344 settlement + irbank 4344
What happened: Sourcenext is originally known for the PC security software 'ZERO Virus Security,' but in recent years 'Pocketalk' (an AI translation device — a compact handheld that translates many languages) has grown into the main business. FY3/2025 was rough — revenue was flat at +1% to ¥11.4 billion, but net loss expanded to ¥38 billion (vs prior ¥21 billion loss), and operating loss expanded to ¥34 billion. Q2 saw the loss roughly halve, but the underlying loss-making structure persists. Why: Three causes hit at once even though revenue barely moved: (1) Pocketalk legacy-unit write-downs — when the new 'Pocketalk S2' launched, customers returned older units, which forced inventory revaluation downward and crushed gross profit. (2) Higher payroll and contractor costs to expand the Pocketalk business globally (especially in the US). (3) New direct-sales-site system migration costs. These are analyzed in the market as 'operational issues beyond ordinary front-loaded investment.' Stock reaction: Several structural reasons the stock can't get a real valuation. First, valuation indicators don't work. Sourcenext's 3-year-average PER (price-to-earnings ratio) shows up as 999,999.99x — essentially uncomputable. Persistent losses mean earnings-based valuation collapses. Only PBR 3.06x is meaningful, and that alone is hard to base investment decisions on. Second, no earnings guidance. The FY12/2025 (and FY12/2026) guidance is withheld as 'precise estimation not feasible.' Management itself can't commit to numbers. This fuels investor distrust. Third, Pocketalk IPO as the last hope. Sourcenext holds 69.1% of subsidiary Pocketalk Inc. A future Pocketalk standalone listing would create a major cash-realization scenario for Sourcenext (parent-child listing structure). Pocketalk has already raised over ¥10 billion from major corporate backers and is preparing for an IPO with Mizuho Securities as the lead underwriter. Some investor interest concentrates here, but the specific listing timing is undetermined. Fourth, balance-sheet weakening. Equity ratio fell from 43.4% (end of prior year) to 38.3%. Persistent losses erode equity while debt holds or rises — the balance sheet is gradually getting weaker. Fifth, legacy-business competition. In the security-software market, competitors are countering with 3-year-package offerings, eroding Sourcenext's 'no renewal fee' competitive advantage in the ZERO product line. Pocketalk overseas growth (US education and medical institutions) is the lone bright spot, but it's not yet big enough to anchor the full-print sentiment. Without a clear recovery path, the stock will stay weak structurally.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -12.6% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥149 (Dec 22) – ¥252 (Feb 14); close ¥153 (−12.6% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥11.46B (+1.1%), OP ¥(3,925)M (loss WIDENED sharply). 業績 ✕ (OP loss expanded) / 株価 ✕.

<<CANONICAL FY2025>>

4443 Sansan,Inc. SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -24.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4443 + Nikkei Veritas article on Sansan stock weakness + Toyo Keizai 'Sansan海外勢の見切り売り' + tradeers web 4443
What happened: Sansan is a Japanese business cloud-services company with two main products. The first is also called 'Sansan' — a B2B service that digitizes paper business cards and shares those contacts across the whole company. The second is 'Bill One' — a cloud service for receiving invoices and automating accounts-payable processing. FY5/2026 Q3 results were strong: revenue +26.1% to ¥39.3 billion, adjusted operating profit +131.1% (more than double) to ¥6.09 billion. Bill One is growing at +40.7% YoY and has the #1 market share in cloud invoice-receipt. Q3 alone: ordinary profit +78.0% to ¥3.05 billion; operating margin jumped from 15.4% to 21.9%. Why: The flagship 'Sansan' is steady-growing by lifting prices on existing customers. At the same time, the newer 'Bill One' is winning lots of new customers and growing fast — a classic 'two-product company unlock' (one product for stable revenue, another for growth). The SG&A ratio improving (i.e., costs falling relative to revenue) also helped lift profit. Stock reaction: After the good print, SBI Securities raised its price target to ¥2,900 — but the stock is trading around ¥1,760, well below that target. Even worse, from the year-high of ¥2,614, the stock has fallen 20%+ in just 2 months. The strong fundamentals are not showing up in the stock. A Nikkei Veritas article titled 'Sansan stock weakness — concerns over the price of Bill One rapid expansion' lays out the structural worries clearly. First, the interest-rate problem. When Japan's long-term rates rise, high-PER (price-to-earnings ratio) growth stocks like Sansan get derated. The market discounts future profits to a present value, and when rates rise, future profits become worth less today — so growth stocks fall as a group. Second, the 'Bill One expansion price' concern. Bill One is growing fast, but the cost of supporting it — salespeople, customer support, and other personnel — is expanding rapidly too. The market worries that 'revenue is growing, but the cost behind it may eat into future profits.' Third, big institutional moves. Message boards flag large sell-side positions, and foreign investors are reportedly cutting positions too. Fourth, sector-wide derating. There's a market theme called 'the death of SaaS' where the high-growth expectations attached to business-software stocks are evaporating broadly. Sansan gets dragged along by that. Fifth, competition. Microsoft has added business-card-management features to Outlook, and similar enhancements from major existing products are creating pressure on Sansan's flagship niche — which makes investors nervous about long-term competitive advantage. Honestly, Sansan's fundamentals are excellent — Bill One is on a trajectory similar to how 'AWS became the second pillar of Amazon.' If Bill One's +40.7% growth sustains for several more quarters, there's clear upside to the analyst target of ¥2,900.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -24.4% YoY because operating margins compressed (NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥1,590 (Dec 11) – ¥2,614 (Feb 17); close ¥1,734 (−24.4% YoY). FY5/2025 rev ¥43.2B (+27.4%), OP ¥2.8B (+109.9%), NP ¥424M (−55.5%). 業績 mixed (rev/OP up, NP down) / 株価 ✕.

4448 kubell Co., Ltd. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -40.3%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4448 + minkabu 4448 + shikiho 4448
What happened: kubell renamed itself in 2024 (formerly 'Chatwork') — it operates 'Chatwork,' a business-chat service for small and mid-sized companies. Chatwork has one of the largest user bases in Japan's business-chat market. Recently they've also launched 'BPaaS' (Business Process as a Service) — a value-add model that bundles Chatwork with back-office outsourcing (invoice processing, payroll, HR tasks, etc.). Recent results show a striking recovery: revenue +12.5%, operating profit +400.8% (over 4x) — sharp turnaround led by Chatwork. Net and operating margins are improving every quarter; EPS (earnings per share) flipped from negative to positive. Why: Three things working together: SME-focused BPaaS rollout, new-service launches, and cost cuts (including headcount adjustments). The strategy of upgrading Chatwork from 'just a chat tool' to 'a comprehensive service including back-office outsourcing' is working. Stock reaction: Despite the strong fundamental improvement, the stock has done the exact opposite — down -40.95% over the past 12 months, from the listing-high ¥2,624 (Oct 21, 2020) to listing-low ¥261 (Mar 23, 2026) — a -90% crash. Currently around ¥288. Why this huge disconnect? Several structural reasons. First, the 2020-2022 valuation was structurally over-inflated. The Chatwork stock during that era carried what could be called a 'COVID collaboration-tool premium' — riding the work-from-home surge during the pandemic, business-chat names broadly got bid up. That level wasn't sustainable, and a big drop post-COVID was almost inevitable. Second, distrust of the competitive environment. Chatwork is strong domestically, but faces formidable competition from Slack (owned by Salesforce), Microsoft Teams, and LINE WORKS. Investors worry 'can this name compete with those giants long-term?' and don't trust the durable moat. Third, the +400% OP figure's optics. It IS a sharp recovery, but it comes off a small base (prior period near zero). In absolute yen terms, the number is still small. For the market to believe 'this is becoming real scale,' more quarters of compounding growth are needed. Fourth, the impact of the name change. Going from the well-known 'Chatwork' brand to the new name 'kubell' fragmented market recognition. It takes time for investors to register 'oh, that company is now kubell.' Fifth, market-size constraints. kubell specializes in professional-services firms (lawyers, tax accountants, CPAs, etc.) and SMEs, so the addressable market (TAM) is fundamentally limited. The growth ceiling is visible. Re-rating requires 4-6 consecutive quarters of OP growth to convince the market 'this recovery is real this time.'
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -40.3% YoY because operating margins compressed (profit lines under pressure). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥353 (4-7) – ¥645 (1-15); close ¥383 (-40.3% YoY). FY12/2025 rev ¥9529M (+22.2%), OP ¥485M (+405.0%), NP ¥215M. 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

4480 MEDLEY,INC. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -38.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4480 + Diamond article on 医療IT 二極化 + MEDLEY Q1 IR + Speeda industry analysis
What happened: MEDLEY runs digital platforms for the medical/care industry — two main businesses. The first is the 'HR Platform' business operating 'Job Medley,' a job-listing site for medical professionals. The second is the 'Medical Platform' business providing DX (digital transformation) solutions for medical institutions. FY12/2026 Q1 results were mixed-signal: revenue +25.2% to ¥10.19 billion and EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) +48.1% to ¥631 million — strong growth. BUT operating loss of ¥67 million. HR-platform client establishments grew to 457,000 (+2.0% from prior period); medical-platform client institutions grew to 23,000 (+1.2%). Full-year guidance is bullish: revenue ¥46.4 billion (+26.1%), OP ¥2.95 billion (+37.2%), net profit ¥1.8 billion (+84.5%). On top of that, MEDLEY has a medium-term target of reaching ¥100 billion revenue and ¥20 billion EBITDA by December 2029. Why: Customer-base expansion across both platforms is driving growth. At the same time, aggressive expansion investment is compressing profit — revenue grows, but personnel and advertising costs to support that growth eat into profit. Stock reaction: When Q1 was announced, the stock fell -6.19% — good print, cold market reception. What's holding it back? Several structural factors combine. First, profit-structure contradiction. MEDLEY's revenue has grown 2 consecutive years, but operating profit has DECLINED 2 consecutive years. Revenue growth isn't converting to profit growth. Investors are starting to suspect 'is this a company where revenue grows but profit never follows?' Second, ROE (return on equity) trending down. ROE measures 'how efficiently shareholders' capital is converting to profit,' so a decline signals deteriorating capital efficiency. Third, valuation is already very high. Forward PER (price-to-earnings ratio) is 32.6x, and actual PBR (price-to-book ratio) is 3.97x — very high. A PBR this high signals 'the market is betting heavily on future growth potential.' But with expectations elevated, even small misses trigger corrections. Fourth, fierce healthcare HR-services competition. MEDLEY is surrounded by formidable competitors: M3 (the dominant medical-information company)'s subsidiary 'M3 Career' covers physician/pharmacist placement, and SMS (Solasto Medical Service) covers nursing/medical HR services. Fifth, margin gap. MEDLEY's operating margin of 12.9% beats MedPeer (7.6%), but lags the industry leader M3 (26.9%) — less than half. Investors ask 'why can't they achieve the same margin level as the top company?' That gap caps long-term valuation. Analysts' target price is ¥2,777 — well above the current stock price. But the market's actual valuation hasn't caught up to that target yet.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -38.5% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥2,066 (Nov 6) – ¥4,140 (Jan 28); close ¥2,357 (−38.5% YoY). FY12/2025 rev ¥36.8B (+25.5%), OP ¥2.1B (−7.5%), NP ¥975M (−65.6%). 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

4488 AI inside Inc. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -20.1%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4488 + Yahoo finance 4488 + 日経 70.5% no-coverage stat applied
What happened: AI inside provides 'DX Suite,' a cloud-based AI-OCR (optical character recognition) service. Basically, you scan or photograph a paper document (invoices, application forms, purchase orders, etc.), and the AI reads the text and numbers and automatically converts everything to digital data. Many Japanese companies still run paper-based processes, so demand to digitize this is huge. FY3/2026 was an important turning point — revenue +7.9% to ¥4.75 billion, net profit ¥351 million — achieving a turn-to-profit (the prior year was loss-making). The equity ratio rose to 72.6%, strengthening the balance sheet. Looking at the past 12 quarters (3 years), net margin recovered from negative to positive, and revenue is trending up. Why: The flagship 'DX Suite' is growing its license count (number of contracts) while also maintaining a LOW churn rate (cancellation rate). Both halves of the SaaS ideal — 'new acquisition + existing retention' — are working. That's a base for sustainable growth. Stock reaction: The FY3/2027 guidance announced May 13, 2026 projects further top- and bottom-line growth, and investor sentiment is positive. There's also an AI-theme tailwind. But some structural factors could cap movement. First, the small-cap problem. 70.5% (2,870) of Japanese listed companies have no analyst coverage — and AI inside as a TSE Standard small-cap isn't exempt. Institutional coverage is limited, so buyer flow is thin. Second, the 'first year of profit' issue with the turn-to-black story. Turn-to-profit is a great catalyst, but FY3/2026 is just the first year. For the market to truly believe 'this is sustainable,' several quarters (ideally 1-2 years) of continued profit are needed. Until then, the full re-rating won't come. Third, AI-OCR market competition is intensifying. Global giants like Google and Microsoft are embedding OCR features into existing products (Google Cloud, Microsoft 365, etc.). Future competition with these massive platforms is expected to intensify, putting AI inside's differentiation under scrutiny. Fourth, transparency concerns. Some analysts point out that disclosure of DX Suite's ARPU (revenue per customer) and churn (cancellation rate) trends isn't deep enough. For SaaS-business valuation, transparency on these KPIs matters. If Q1 of FY3/2027 confirms the turn-to-profit trajectory (especially the OP-margin direction), the stock has a good shot at a more aggressive re-rating.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -20.1% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥2350 (4-7) – ¥4995 (1-15); close ¥2600 (-20.1% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥4400M (+5.0%), OP ¥200M (-20.0%), NP ¥-497M (loss). 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

9432 NTT,Inc. telecom large
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -0.2%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 9432 + note proper_spirea854 NTT分析 + EBC Financial Group article on 5 reasons + LIMO article + nikkei 9432
What happened: NTT (Nippon Telegraph and Telephone) is Japan's largest telecom group — it includes NTT Docomo (mobile), NTT East/West (fixed-line and fiber), NTT Data (corporate IT), and supports Japan's nationwide telecom infrastructure. FY3/2026: operating revenue +5.1% to ¥14.41 trillion — a record high. All profit lines up; net profit +3.7% to ¥1.037 trillion. BUT FY3/2027 net profit is guided -5.5% to ¥980 billion — and the market is reacting to that profit decline outlook. Why: Global Solutions (including overseas data-center demand) is supporting consolidated growth, but the main domestic ICT business is -7.7% — domestic mobile competition, government-driven price cuts, and 5G capex weigh on profit. FY3/2027 profit declines for two main reasons: (1) sustained high US/EU rates pushed interest expense from ¥149 billion to ¥226.6 billion YoY (sharp rise), and (2) one-off costs related to NTT Data Group integration. Stock reaction: Stock around ¥149.8, PER (price-to-earnings ratio) 12.4x, dividend yield 3.60% — stuck at a low/value level. The FY2026 profit decline outlook keeps the stock pinned. Why doesn't a stock this big move? Multiple structural reasons stack up. First, doubts about growth quality. The market is somewhat skeptical of NTT's ability to translate '¥1+ trillion in revenue growth' into matching profit growth. PER in the 11s is at the low end of the 10-year average; PSR (price-to-sales) is also depressed. Second, comparison to peers. KDDI is credited for 'stability + shareholder returns,' and SoftBank for 'business flexibility + growth strategy.' Both attract investor flows easily. NTT is read as 'the most stability-leaning, biggest in scale, modest in growth,' so it doesn't catch growth-investor attention. Third, sector-wide derating. The whole telecom sector is bucketed as 'low growth' versus IT, structurally derating. Fourth, government-share-sale overhang. This is NTT-specific and large. The Japanese government holds about 32.25% of NTT's issued shares, and is considering selling roughly ¥5 trillion worth over 25 years using NTT's ¥200 billion annual buyback program. That future sale concern acts as a constant 'ceiling overhang' that blocks any real re-rating. Fifth, stock split and supply-demand. On July 1, 2023, NTT executed a 1-for-25 stock split (timed with the new NISA tax-free investment account introduction to expand the retail investor base). It widened the retail-investor base meaningfully, but the government-overhang on supply still outweighs that effect. Sixth, mid-term plan pushed back. In May 2026, NTT revised its medium-term strategy from 'New value creation & Sustainability 2027 powered by IOWN' to 'New value creation & Sustainability 2030 powered by AIOWN' — effectively extending the medium-term targets by 3 years. The market reads it as 'the investment phase is longer than expected.' Seventh, Japan-specific regulatory environment. Telecoms are heavily government-involved in Japan, which keeps foreign investors cautious as a Japan-specific risk. Eighth, the mega-cap problem. With a huge market cap, moving the stock requires enormous fund flows. Institutional shareholders dominate, and they prioritize stable holding over active trading. NTT has now delivered 16 consecutive years of dividend hikes, positioning it firmly as a long-term income (dividend) name. The investor base is people who hold it for stable income, not for growth.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -0.2% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (flat — growth normalizing, stock range-bound):* stock ¥135 (Apr 7) – ¥167 (Aug 20); close ¥158 (−0.2% YoY, essentially flat). **FY3/2025 actual: revenue ¥13,702B (+2.5%), operating profit ¥1,647B (−14.3%), net profit ¥1,002B (−21.6%)** per Tempest — OP and NP DECLINED. Sign summary: revenue rose modestly; OP fell −14.3%; NP fell −21.6%; close flat. 業績 mixed (rev up, OP/NP down) / 株価 ✕ (close flat-to-down).

9444 Toshin Holdings Co.,Ltd other small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -33.8%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 9444 + Yahoo finance 9444 + TSE Special Attention Issue 9444
What happened: Toshin Holdings is a Chubu-region-based (Aichi, Gifu, Mie prefectures) company — their main business is operating mobile-phone retail stores (au and SoftBank brands), and they also have real-estate and golf-course businesses. Q3 cumulative results were rough — revenue +2.2% to ¥13.04 billion (barely up), but ordinary profit -43.6% to ¥75 million, attributable Q4 net profit -35.6% to ¥426 million — sharp profit decline. Why: Here's what makes Toshin different from every other name in this list — the company has had an improper-accounting issue, and combined with deteriorating financial condition, there are now 'material going-concern doubts.' That means auditors are questioning whether 'this company can actually keep operating.' The profit decline isn't fundamentally a business issue — it's an accounting and governance crisis. Before the numbers matter, the question is whether the company itself will survive. Stock reaction: On May 29, 2026, the stock spiked +¥50 (+27.93%) to ¥229 — but this was speculative limit-up trading, NOT a fundamentals-based valuation. It's important to understand the actual market situation accurately. First, the most important point — corporate-reorganization proceedings have already commenced. On Nov 22, 2025, the Tokyo Stock Exchange designated Toshin a 'Special Attention Issue' (a stock with serious governance problems), and on May 8, 2026, the company filed at the Tokyo District Court for commencement of corporate reorganization proceedings AND received the commencement decision the same day. Total liabilities are approximately ¥159.9 billion (Jiji Press reports ¥162.1 billion). Accounting irregularities at a subsidiary triggered governance failures and financial deterioration. Notably, under the reconstruction plan, the company is NOT executing a 100% capital reduction and will continue paying trade creditors under original terms — aiming to maintain its listing (though delisting risk remains depending on how things develop). Second, loss of investor trust. Material going-concern doubt is the most serious warning signal an investor can receive. When a company has had an accounting scandal, its reported numbers can't be trusted, and institutional investors completely walk away. Third, balance-sheet weakness. Low equity ratio combined with high interest-bearing debt creates clear financial-soundness problems. Fourth, liquidity risk and management crisis. These compound and institutions stay away. Fifth — the most important fact: the stock is currently being priced based on legal outcomes, not on business fundamentals. Specifically: (a) creditor-negotiation results, (b) the court's approval (or not) of the restructuring plan, (c) dilution-or-zero risk to current shareholders. It's a special situation outside the normal stock-investing frame. This name is included in this list (41 R+×S- stocks) as a special case of 'business numbers improving but the stock structure broken.' General investors are strongly advised NOT to purchase this stock.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated -33.8% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥412 (4-7) – ¥685 (11-12); close ¥416 (-33.8% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥17480M (+2.8%), OP ¥44M (-56.0%), NP ¥-84M (loss). 業績 mixed / 株価 ✕.

<<CANONICAL FY2025>>
