9468 KADOKAWA CORPORATION content_games mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: +1.0%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 9468 + Advertimes 9468 article + Branc article on KADOKAWA + Note KADOKAWA 戦略分析
What happened: KADOKAWA is a major Japanese entertainment conglomerate — they're in publishing (light novels, manga, magazines), anime production, game development, education/EdTech (operating N High School and others), and IP (intellectual property) creation, across many businesses. FY3/2026 full-year results were rough: revenue +1.8% to ¥282.91 billion (essentially flat), operating profit -51.3% to ¥8.10 billion (less than half of last year), ordinary profit -34.1% to ¥11.70 billion, net profit also down sharply. By segment, the flagship Publishing & IP Creation business saw OP fall -51.6%, the Anime & Live-Action Video segment swung to a loss, while Education / EdTech was the lone bright spot with revenue +13.5% and OP +19.4%. The FY3/2027 guide is for OP +24.7% to ¥10.1 billion. Why: Two big problems hit at once. (1) Publishing / IP-creation business challenges: Domestic book sales are weak. Even though they increased the number of titles published, that didn't translate to more hits. Marketing and promo resources got scattered across too many titles, so each one's visibility weakened. Plus, over-reliance on the proven 'narō / isekai' genre (web-novel-origin reincarnation stories from the 'Narō' platform) led to genre saturation. (2) Anime business challenges: Lack of major hit titles. Plus, they have their own anime studios in-house, and the front-loaded investment burden is heavy. Industry-wide, competition for hit-source rights has intensified and production costs are rising sharply. Stock reaction: The stock fell sharply on the profit-decline announcement. Why? Multiple structural problems are combining. First, sector-wide derating. The publishing and anime industries are slowing broadly, so even when KADOKAWA has good news, sector headwinds drown it out. Second, intensifying competition. In anime, fighting for production-line capacity (studio slots) drives production costs up sharply. The anime cycle is 'invest now, collect over years' — that cycle is getting longer, making valuation harder for investors. Third, streaming-platform demand shift. There used to be an era where 'just make anime and Netflix or Amazon Prime Video would pay top dollar for the license.' Now streaming platforms are pickier about what they buy, and mid-tier titles can't expect the licensing income they used to. That's an industry-wide business-model shift. Fourth, diversified-business complexity. KADOKAWA spans publishing, anime, games, education/EdTech, live events, and more — each moves differently, making the institutional-analysis model complex. It also makes ongoing analyst coverage harder. Fifth, guidance-credibility risk. A -51% YoY OP decline of the magnitude just printed damages market trust by itself. Investors ask 'can we trust the FY3/2027 guide of ¥10.1 billion either, or will it get cut again?' Sixth, capital-efficiency issues. ROE (return on equity) and ROA (return on assets) are below conventional thresholds — profitability is unstable. Recovery requires (a) a new flagship hit (something at the level of 'Demon Slayer' or 'Sword Art Online'), or (b) concrete success in overseas IP expansion.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
Gap framing: Revenue kept growing in 2025 on continued demand for the core business, BUT the stock derated +1.0% YoY because operating margins compressed (OP down and NP down). The gap is the market pricing in the margin pressure ahead of any margin recovery — recoverable if margins normalize, harder to break if the compression is structural.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025:* stock ¥3,005 (Nov 7) – ¥4,117 (Jun 24); close ¥3,176 (+1.0% YoY). FY3/2025 rev ¥277.9B (+7.7%), OP ¥16.7B (−9.7%), NP ¥7.4B (−35.1%). 業績 mixed (rev up, OP/NP down) / 株価 ◎ (close +1.0% flat).

<<CANONICAL FY2025>>

<<END>>
Loss-making SaaS de-rated — market stopped paying for "growth-at-any-cost" 4 names
In plain terms: high-PER SaaS companies that grew revenue strongly but kept posting losses or thin margins. The market stopped tolerating "growth at any cost" and rotated capital out of the cohort. Examples in this bucket: Money Forward, freee, JMDC, Appier Group. Rule used: ticker-specific override for documented loss-making / thin-margin SaaS names. Why this matters: this is a cohort-wide derating, not company-specific failure. Industry commentary even labels it "SaaSの死" (the death of SaaS) — investors shifted from "growth-only" to "Rule of 40" (revenue growth + operating margin ≥ 40%) as the new bar. If these companies pivot to profitability or hit the Rule of 40 milestone, the cohort can re-rate together. What to watch: management language shifting from growth-only to profitability, the first quarter of sustained GAAP profit, and broker upgrades that follow a clean margin print.
3994 Money Forward,Inc. SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -3.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3994 + Yahoo finance 3994 (Tokai Tokyo downgrade article) + Money Forward IR + Buffett Code SaaS accounting article
What happened: Money Forward makes cloud accounting, HR/labor, and expense-management software for small and mid-sized companies — basically, they put boring back-office work into the cloud. FY11/2026 Q1 (Dec 2025 - Feb 2026): revenue +25.3% to ¥14.67 billion, SaaS ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue — the predictable subscription revenue) +34.2% to ¥44.3 billion. Operating profit FLIPPED TO POSITIVE at ¥168 million (vs prior-year loss of ¥-580 million); net profit ¥1.83 billion (vs prior year's ¥-1.12 billion loss). The Business segment's ARR grew +36.8%, and Fintech-related ARR grew an impressive +90%. Why: Analysts had expected a massive operating loss of over ¥5.6 billion — instead Money Forward delivered actual operating profit. That's a 'massive beat'. Years of upfront investment (engineer hiring, sales expansion, building their platform) finally clicked into operating leverage (the dynamic where, once you cross a certain scale, profit grows faster than revenue). The long-awaited 'becoming profitable' inflection point has arrived. Stock reaction: The fundamentals are improving, but several structural problems are happening at once and keeping the stock pinned. The big news first — Tokai Tokyo Intelligence Lab downgraded its investment rating from 'Outperform' (beat the market) to 'Neutral', and cut its price target from ¥6,900 to ¥4,200. Their reason: 'Customer acquisition in the core Business segment is strong, but ARPU (revenue per customer) is rising slowly even after price hikes, and the non-Business segments aren't growing fast enough — so meaningful operating-profit acquisition is still pushed out into the future.' Valuation is the next problem — Money Forward's PER (price-to-earnings ratio — how many years of current profit the stock costs) is about 137x. That's extremely high. Its PSR (price-to-sales ratio) is 4.3x. Unless profit grows at 50%+ per year for several years, valuations this high are very hard to justify. With expectations sky-high, even small disappointments trigger sharp selling. Competition is fierce too. Money Forward and freee are running neck-and-neck on ARR scale and growth rate, so both are bidding for the same customers — that makes margin improvement hard for either of them. On top of that, in May 2026 Money Forward had a bank-account-connection service outage (a security incident), plus a separate GitHub unauthorized-access incident. Both raised concerns about compliance and trust. Shareholder mix also plays a role — about 41% foreign institutional and 18% domestic institutional, so roughly 60% institutional ownership. Those investors move based on relative-portfolio decisions, so when expectations slip even a little, they shift quickly to selling pressure. The trigger for further re-rating — Q2 and Q3 need to show quarterly profitability sustains, and ARPU growth needs to accelerate. If they prove Q1 wasn't a one-off, the next leg of re-rating can begin.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):

Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (first-ever NP positive — operational inflection delivered, stock ambivalent):* stock ¥3,330 (Apr 9) – ¥6,794 (Aug 19); year-end close ¥4,668 (−3.4% YoY). **FY11/2025 actual (announced January 2026 — POST-calendar-window): revenue ¥50,350M (+24.7% YoY), operating loss ¥(2,653)M (loss narrowed from ¥(4,735)M), net profit ¥1,587M per senior-verified primary source (FIRST-EVER FULL-YEAR NET PROFIT IN COMPANY HISTORY)**; irbank.net reports NP ¥2,463M (consolidated vs parent-attributable line-item difference); Tempest reports profit ¥1,587M matching senior. Operating loss continued to narrow; net profit turn-to-black achieved on a combination of operating-loss narrowing + one-off positive non-operating items per management commentary. Stock action during calendar 2025: massive intra-year recovery (+104% from Apr 9 low ¥3,330 to Aug 19 high ¥6,794) on quarterly trajectory data as Q1/Q2/Q3 results showed continued ARR growth + operating-loss narrowing — but stock gave back gains by year-end (close ¥4,668, −3.4% YoY) suggesting market skepticism about durability of the inflection. Sign summary: revenue rose +24.7% YoY; operating loss narrowed +¥2.1B; **net profit first-ever positive ¥1,587M from prior net loss ¥6,331M**; shares had +104% mid-year rally (Apr → Aug) on quarterly inflection signal then gave back gains (close −3.4% YoY). 業績 ◎ (NP first-ever positive + revenue acceleration + OP loss narrowing) / 株価 mixed (close down −3.4% YoY but mid-year +104% rally; year-end ambivalent on inflection durability).

4180 Appier Group, Inc.
Revenue: n/a Stock 2025 YoY: n/a
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4180 + note deb_note Appier analysis + Yahoo finance 4180 forum + plaza.rakuten boso2323 + nikkeiyosoku crash 4180
What happened: Appier Group is a Taiwan-headquartered AI marketing-platform company — they provide software that uses AI to optimize digital ad delivery and make companies' marketing operations more efficient. The company is HQ'd in Taiwan and listed on TSE Mothers (now TSE Growth) in 2021. They have a global presence with operations in Taiwan, Singapore, Japan, US, etc. FY12/2025 numbers were excellent: revenue +28.4% to ¥43.74 billion, ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) +33.1% to ¥48.26 billion, EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) +39.4% to ¥6.85 billion, operating profit +50.2% to ¥2.98 billion. But net profit fell -12.6% to ¥2.56 billion because of M&A (acquisitions) and expansion costs. FY12/2026 Q1 also strong: revenue +29.4% to ¥12.10 billion, OP +153.4% to ¥185 million. From 2020 to 2024, revenue is up 3.8x with a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of about 30% — a textbook high-growth company. Why: AI marketing-platform demand is real, growth continues globally with double-digit numbers. The M&A-led growth strategy weighs temporarily on net profit through acquisition-related costs and goodwill amortization (this is a strategic choice). Stock reaction: Even with growth like this, the stock is down ~70% from listing. Fundamentals are great, but the stock has crashed. Multiple structural factors combine. First, macro headwinds. The global 2021-2023 rate hikes broadly sold growth stocks. Post-IPO excessive expectations + competition intensification + M&A costs combined to trigger the valuation adjustment. Second, excessive expectation → reality adjustment. At IPO, Appier carried a strong 'AI×SaaS / Asian unicorn' narrative, with the stock trading at a high PSR (price-to-sales ratio). Rate hikes and market cooling stripped that narrative premium. Third, intensifying competition. In the Martech (marketing-technology) sector, giants like Salesforce and Adobe overlap on some functions. With Appier doing many acquisitions, post-deal integration work, data-linkage costs, and development-cost increases are near-term concerns the market watches. Fourth, valuation. Appier's forward PER (price-to-earnings ratio) is 28.6x — still high even after the drop. Growth expectations are already priced in, so small misses bring sell pressure. Fifth, weak supply-demand and limited institutional interest. Forum chatter flags 'short institutions coordinating to keep the price down' and 'supply-demand too weak.' Short sellers are likely an active cap on upside. The business is clearly strengthening, but macro environment + valuation adjustment + sector factors + flow factors + high PER all combine to suppress the stock.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
2025: stock flat/—; bucket=saas_derated.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (record-year #5 — operating margin expanding):* stock ¥991 (Nov 19) – ¥1,875 (Feb 14); Nov 2025 ¥991 was a new multi-year low (only marginally above 2024 Aug ¥928). **FY12/2025 actual (announced ~Feb 2026): revenue ¥43,737M (+28.4% YoY), operating profit ¥2,976M (+50.2%), ARR ¥48,259M (+33.1%), EBITDA ¥6,854M (+39.4%), net profit ¥2,558M (-12.6% on tax/other), operating margin expanding** per IR + ARR data; 5 consecutive years of record-high operating profit. Sign summary: revenue rose +28.4% YoY; operating profit rose +50.2%; ARR rose +33.1%; EBITDA rose +39.4%; operating margin expanding; shares fell to ¥991 multi-year low (Nov 19) despite the +50% OP growth — stock didn't recognize the continued acceleration. 業績 ◎◎ (record OP + margin expansion + ARR acceleration) / 株価 ✕ (still under-priced de-rated grower).

Cautious side: stock still well below the 2021 IPO peak ¥2,559 (Nov 2025 low ¥991 = -61% off ATH); the 2021 peak was post-IPO mania at a level unjustified by the loss-making business at the time, so it may not be a meaningful target; net profit declined -12.6% in FY25 despite strong OP — needs human verification on whether tax effect or operational issue; competitive AI-SaaS pressure from larger global players (Salesforce, Adobe AI tools).

Sources:

4478 freee K.K. SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -1.0%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4478 + Nikkei Veritas first-profit crash article + minkabu 4478 + freee 通期予想 documentation
What happened: freee makes cloud accounting, HR/labor, and sales-management SaaS for small and mid-sized companies — which puts them in nearly direct competition with Money Forward. FY6/2026 Q3 cumulative revenue grew +29.3% to ¥30.84 billion — strong growth. Platform ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) grew +23.2% to ¥42.48 billion. But profit fell YoY because of heavy upfront investment. Full-year guidance was raised, so high growth is expected to continue. Why: freee is sticking with its SaaS-expansion strategy and openly prioritizes growth over near-term profit. Investments in engineer hiring, feature expansion, and enterprise (large-company) sales push down profit — but the customer base and feature depth keep expanding as a result. Stock reaction: Here's the essential lesson — at the prior-year results (FY8/2025), freee achieved its first ever net profit since founding. Normally that would be a major positive catalyst for the stock. The result was the opposite — the stock cratered after earnings. A Nikkei Veritas article titled 'freee stock crashes despite first profitability — disappointment over flat-margin guide' explains the puzzle clearly. The reason: behind the 'first profitability' headline, the FY2026 outlook implied a FLAT margin profile vs the prior year. The stock fell over 14% in the two days following earnings. The market had wanted to celebrate 'crossing into profitability,' but realized there was no step-function margin improvement coming next — so it sold off in disappointment. For reference, the FY2026 full-year guide is revenue ¥40.9-41.5 billion (+23-25% YoY) with ARR expected to top ¥42.0 billion — solid numbers, but market expectations had been higher. Investors wanted 'a full transition from growth phase to harvesting phase,' but the reality was still 'investment phase continues.' Several structural problems compound this. First, the whole business-software sector has a theme called 'the death of SaaS,' and old high-growth expectations are evaporating. Second, freee's 29.3% revenue growth is still solid, but profit growth has slowed because of front-loaded investment — so the gap between actual results and market expectations is wide. Third, the whole SaaS / cloud industry is undergoing valuation derating, so even if freee's own results are good, it gets dragged along. Fourth, in the Growth-stock market investors have shifted to taking profits in large-cap names. Fifth — and this is a sharp one — the contrast with Money Forward (3994), freee's main rival. Money Forward delivered an operating-profit turnaround in Q1 of April 2026 and surged nearly limit-up (+18%). That contrast made the market's read on freee even harsher. For freee to be re-rated meaningfully, its future guidance needs to show step-function margin improvement — not a flat margin, but a clear upward curve.

4483 JMDC Inc. SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: +1.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4483 + minkabu 4483 + Nikkei JMDC article + shikiho JMDC article on public offering + DZH 個別株情報 article
What happened: JMDC handles one of the largest medical big-data assets in Japan — they collect anonymized medical claims (insurance reimbursement) data from health insurance unions, then sell that data for pharmaceutical-company marketing, insurance underwriting, and medical research. They also operate a telemedicine (online consultation) platform. Q3 results were excellent: revenue +23.2% to ¥36.49 billion, operating profit +37.1% to ¥7.77 billion — strong growth. The flagship Health-Big-Data segment led at +26.4%. Q3 cumulative net profit +24.6% to ¥4.81 billion. Why: Both the Health-Big-Data segment and the telemedicine segment grew. Data-sales businesses have a special feature: 'marginal margin' (the profit boost from each additional yen of revenue) is very high. Once the data is collected, selling it to one more company costs almost nothing additional. So revenue growth converts directly to profit growth. That's JMDC's strength. Stock reaction: Strong fundamentals, but multiple structural pressures on the stock. First, the expectation-gap problem. Q3 cumulative progress against the full-year guide of ¥7.4 billion is only 65.0% — below the 5-year-average 67.1%. Plus, the FY3/2026 OP guidance of ¥11.5 billion (+31.9%) is BELOW the QUICK consensus (analyst average) of ¥11.72 billion. Ordinary profit guidance ¥11.0 billion (+29.3%) is also BELOW the consensus ¥11.57 billion. So it's a 'good print' but 'not as good as the market expected.' Second, analyst rating cuts. Multiple analysts have been cutting price targets on JMDC, and institutional sentiment is worsening. (Note: one earlier report suggested a US major brokerage cut its target to ¥3,400, but re-verification couldn't directly confirm that specific figure, so it should be checked against the source if cited.) Third, a 'material exhaustion' feeling. The gap to target is 78.18% (significant upside on paper), but a pattern has emerged where upward revisions actually trigger selling. Growth expectations are already priced in, and there's no visible next surprise. Fourth, investor candor on message boards: 'The stock hasn't moved at all in 10 years.' There's a structural long-term-deadlock dynamic in play. Fifth, capital-allocation concerns. The equity ratio is declining and interest-bearing debt is rising, raising balance-sheet caution. Sixth, niche specialized sector. Medical data is a specialized field, so institutional interest is limited and the whole sub-sector is also derating. Seventh, supply-demand worsening from a recent public offering / third-party allotment. New share issuance dilutes per-share value, hurting existing shareholders.

Orphan — strong fundamentals but no analyst coverage 4 names
In plain terms: companies whose business is performing well — but almost no broker covers them and institutional ownership is near zero. The stock drifts because there is simply nobody telling the story to capital allocators. Examples in this bucket: HENNGE, Cybozu, TechMatrix, JAPAN MATERIAL. Rule used: ticker-specific override for names with documented coverage gaps and small institutional ownership. Why this matters: about 70% of TSE-listed companies have ZERO analyst coverage (industry stat). When growth is durable but nobody writes about it, the stock can stay cheap for years. But the trigger is mechanical — when ONE broker initiates coverage with a Buy, the pool of potential buyers expands overnight and the stock can re-rate sharply. What to watch: new broker initiations, IR-activity ramp-up (English presentations, overseas NDRs), TSE PBR-improvement disclosure progress, and any change in cross-shareholding that could free up float.
3762 TechMatrix Corporation SI_IT_services small
Revenue: n/a Stock 2025 YoY: n/a
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3762 + Yahoo finance 3762 + 株予報Pro 3762
What happened: TechMatrix is an independent Japanese IT company providing corporate IT infrastructure — it runs two main businesses: 'Information Infrastructure' (selling and maintaining security products and networking gear) and 'CRM' (solutions for customer-service contact centers). FY3/2026 was a record-breaker on both lines: revenue +10.6% to ¥71.7 billion, operating profit +16.5% to ¥7.76 billion. The Information Infrastructure business is strong, especially cloud-security products winning new contracts and renewals. Why: Steady durable execution in the two core businesses — no flash, but reliably stacks earnings year after year. This is a textbook 'compounder' (a company that grows steadily and compounds). It just delivered 11 consecutive years of record profit. Stock reaction: The fundamentals are excellent, but the stock has been weak post-earnings. A few structural reasons for the lack of re-rating. First, financial-statement concerns. TechMatrix's equity ratio (the portion of assets that isn't debt) is below the conventional 30% threshold considered healthy. On top of that, interest-bearing debt is trending up. Both of these factors weigh negatively on institutional investment decisions — investors who prefer clean balance sheets stay away. Second, valuation. PBR (price-to-book ratio — how many times the company's book value the stock is priced at) is 3.38x, which is relatively high. That means 'the market has already baked in high expectations,' so even minor results misses can trigger corrections (selloffs). Third, sector-perception. Despite 11 consecutive years of record profit, in the IT-services / SI (systems integration) industry, 'compounders without a dramatic narrative' don't catch growth-investor attention. There's no eye-catching theme like AI or data centers attached to TechMatrix, so it doesn't get media coverage and doesn't come up in investor conversations. Fourth, limited global expansion. TechMatrix has very limited overseas business, so there's no catalyst for a global-style re-rating. It tends to stay in the 'understated good Japanese company' bucket valuation-wise. Re-rating triggers — (a) a major dividend hike or buyback that puts it in income-investor portfolios, (b) sector rotation into IT-infrastructure as cybersecurity demand surges, or (c) a spin-off of the cybersecurity business as a standalone listing.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
2025: stock flat/—; bucket=orphan_no_coverage.
Source prose (fixture rawExplanation)
*2025 (record-year confirmation — clean ◎):* FY3/2025 actual (announced May 9, 2025): revenue ¥64,882M (+21.7% YoY, +¥11,578M increase, **record high**), operating profit ¥6,668M (+14.0%, +¥818M), net profit ¥4,060M (+14.7%). **11 consecutive years of record profit** per management context. FY3/2026 guidance: revenue ¥73,000M (+12.5%), OP ¥7,600M (+13.9%), net ¥4,880M (+20.2%). Stock ¥1,720 (Apr 7) – ¥2,584 (Jan 31). Jan 2025 was actually the prior-year-end momentum continuation; through 2025 the stock declined from Jan high to Apr low, then recovered (per the Nikkei range data — high was Jan, low was Apr). Stock-move catalyst: FY3/2025 record results + 11-year streak + raised FY3/2026 guidance + Firmus acquisition contribution. 業績 ◎ on record results + acceleration / 株価 mixed (Jan peak followed by spring decline — profit-taking after the strong 2024 rally is plausible but no source names it).

Cautious side: import-reseller business model dependence on North American technology vendors (FX-sensitive, supplier-relationship risk); Firmus integration risk; concentration in network security as a single growth driver.

Sign summary: revenue rose +21.7% YoY; operating profit rose +14.0%; net profit rose +14.7%; record revenue, record operating profit, 11 consecutive years of record profit; FY3/2026 guidance raised; shares rallied to ¥2,584 high (Jan 31); business strong, stock strong.

4475 HENNGE K.K. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: +1.8%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4475 + HENNGE FY9/2025 決算短信 + HENNGE One IDaaS #1 press release + logmi finance HENNGE article + SBI report on HENNGE
What happened: HENNGE is a Japanese corporate cloud-security company. Their flagship product 'HENNGE One' bundles multiple security features needed for safe cloud use — single sign-on (one login for many services), email encryption, identity/access management — into a single package. In industry terms, this is called IDaaS (Identity as a Service — providing ID management via the cloud). FY9/2025 was excellent: revenue +30.59% to ¥10.92 billion, net profit +64.18% to ¥1.36 billion, ordinary profit +85.4% to ¥1.85 billion. FY9/2026 is expected to continue growing at +11.8% to ¥2.07 billion — which would be 4 consecutive years of record profit. They also achieved 12 consecutive years of revenue growth and 5 consecutive years of profit growth. Q3 alone: ordinary profit was 3x YoY at ¥240 million; operating margin jumped from 4.3% to 7.3%. Why: HENNGE One holds a long-standing #1 vendor position in Japan's IDaaS (Identity as a Service) market (multiple market surveys including ITR have reported HENNGE as #1 for multiple consecutive years — specific year count should be verified against the original press release). The drivers: (1) enterprise cloud adoption is expanding fast, and (2) cyberattacks like ransomware (extortion malware) are getting sophisticated. HENNGE One's unified 'security + usability' solution is being kept by existing customers and chosen by new ones. Contracted users are in the millions, and client companies number 3,731 as of March 31, 2026 — a substantial share of the Japanese enterprise market. Stock reaction: Despite 4 consecutive years of record profit, the stock has several structural valuation caps. First — the most surprising fact: HENNGE has 'Analyst-coverage: none.' Even with this kind of track record, major brokerages don't cover it, and institutional buyers are structurally absent. Second, valuation is already very high. HENNGE's PER (price-to-earnings ratio) is 43.2x; PBR (price-to-book) is 16.6x. Even for a growth stock these are extremely elevated. The market has already priced in high growth, leaving little room for further upside. Third, the broader Growth-market lag. TSE Growth has many domestic-demand names, and while capital concentrates in specific themes like AI hardware, security-service names like HENNGE have relatively low recognition. Fourth, institutional liquidity constraints. Growth-market names have limited market caps, missing many large institutions' minimum-position-size requirements. Even if they wanted to buy, they often can't. Fifth, sector-level derating. Investor capital is flowing to AI-related and data-center names; infrastructure-software security like HENNGE One is relatively derated. In the US, security names like Okta, Cloudflare, Zscaler, and CrowdStrike trade at premium multiples — but in Japan, security names don't get the same treatment. HENNGE is fundamentally a 'quiet winner' — but for the stock to catch up with the business, it may need a new narrative like overseas expansion or AI-related new services.

4776 Cybozu,Inc. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -2.8%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4776 + Cybozu FY12/2025 決算短信 + Business Insider Japan article '時価総額1000億円超...評価されない' + サイボウズ式 article + Nikkei 4776 article on FY2026 guidance
What happened: Cybozu makes business collaboration tools and cloud services — the flagship is 'kintone', a no-code (no programming needed) cloud platform for building business apps. FY12/2025 was big across every metric: revenue +26.1% to ¥37.43 billion, operating profit +106.4% (more than double) to ¥10.10 billion, ordinary profit +93.5% to ¥10.33 billion, net profit +99.2% to ¥7.08 billion. kintone is used by 39,000 domestic companies and brought in ¥21.69 billion (+33.9%). Total cloud client base: 70,000 companies, with 3.6 million user licenses. FY12/2026 guide: revenue +12.7% to ¥42.17 billion, OP +4.1% to ¥10.51 billion. Why: The cloud services business (mostly kintone) keeps adding new client companies. On top of that, price increases and higher per-customer revenue lifted profits. Both the product and the pricing strategy are working. Stock reaction: With fundamentals like these, you'd expect the stock to rip — but it hasn't. This is the long-standing puzzle of Cybozu, the company 'investors don't reward.' A Business Insider Japan article titled 'Cybozu has a market cap over ¥100bn and strong results — why don't investors evaluate it?' addresses this puzzle directly. The reasons all trace back to deliberate management choices. First, disclosure. To value a SaaS business, institutional investors look at metrics like ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue), churn rate (customer cancellation rate), and ARPU (revenue per customer) — these are how you predict future revenue. Cybozu does NOT disclose these. They publish some monthly cloud-revenue figures, but the core SaaS KPIs are missing. So institutions are stuck in 'unable to analyze' mode. Second, IR approach. Most listed companies publish voluntary quarterly earnings materials. Cybozu publishes them only once a year (full-year only). They also refuse to hold one-on-one meetings with institutional investors (citing 'fairness'), and instead hold multi-attendee meetings with publicly disclosed minutes. That format makes it hard for institutions to engage deeply. And the most fundamental point — management philosophy. CEO Aono has historically been described as 'market-averse,' viewed by institutional investors as showing less of the profit and shareholder-return orientation that a listed company is normally expected to demonstrate. BUT an important change — at the FY2023 full-year earnings briefing on February 27, 2024, Aono said 'we've been presenting only convenient information so far' and disclosed SaaS metrics like ARR for the first time. Responding to institutional-investor pressure, Cybozu pivoted its IR approach. The newly disclosed figures revealed ARR growth had declined from over 20% in prior years to 17.5% in FY2023. As the cumulative result of years of this dynamic, Cybozu's PSR (price-to-sales ratio) has consistently traded below Money Forward, freee, AND the SaaS-company average — what's called 'perpetually undervalued' (万年割安) in Japanese. Institutions weren't 'choosing not to value' Cybozu; they 'couldn't value' it given the information they had. The 2024 IR pivot is a positive signal, but closing the historical valuation gap will take time as a structural process.

6055 JAPAN MATERIAL Co., Ltd. SI_IT_services mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -8.9%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 6055 + minkabu 6055 + nikkei 6055 + SBI report on 半導体 関連銘柄
What happened: Japan Material is an infrastructure-services company for semiconductor factories — they provide the on-site essentials at semi fabs: 'high-purity gas' (specialty gases used in manufacturing processes), chemical management, and equipment maintenance. Every time a semi manufacturer builds a new fab, Japan Material's work expands — so the company benefits directly from the semi capex boom. FY3/2026 was strong: revenue +10.1% to ¥57.98 billion, operating profit +30.9% to ¥14.64 billion. Trailing 12 quarters (3 years) show net and OP margins trending up YoY. Why: The main Electronics segment benefits from expanding semiconductor capex and high production activity. Leading-edge semi demand (cutting-edge processes like 2nm, 3nm) is an additional tailwind. Semi manufacturing requires gas, chemicals, and equipment maintenance, so the entire semi-industry growth flows directly into Japan Material's revenue. It's a 'picks-and-shovels' model — no matter which semi maker wins, the company selling the manufacturing infrastructure profits. Stock reaction: Over the past 12 months, Japan Material's stock is +38.94% — strong medium-term action. On May 29, 2026, a mid-tier domestic broker reiterated 'Bullish' and raised the price target to ¥2,400. But even with these strong fundamentals, there are structural factors that keep full valuation recognition from settling. First, small-cap structural challenge. Looking at the data, over the past 5 years Japanese large-caps gained +17.60% while small-caps only gained +5.75% — there's a clear 'size effect' where large-caps get bought more. Huge pools of capital (pension funds, ESG funds, passive index-tracking funds) prefer high-liquidity names with market caps above ¥100 billion. Small-caps like Japan Material don't easily make it into their investable universe. Second, relative position in the sector. As a semi-supplier, Japan Material is much smaller than semi-equipment majors (Tokyo Electron, SCREEN, etc.). Most of its revenue comes from Electronics, with heavy concentration on a few large customers — so those customers' capex plans and utilization swings directly hit results. Third, engineer-hiring constraint. Management cites 'securing skilled engineers' as the #1 expansion challenge. Talent competition in the semi industry is fierce, and external hiring is hard. That structurally caps growth speed. Fourth, institutional coverage gap. As a small-cap, major-broker analyst coverage is thin, and earnings information doesn't fully penetrate the market. Japan Material is settling into a reputation as 'an understated but solid winner' in the semi theme. But for a full re-rating, further catalysts are needed — overseas expansion, new-customer wins, or accelerating ramp-ups of new fabs like TSMC Kumamoto and Rapidus Hokkaido.

AI disrupted — business model directly threatened by generative AI 3 names
In plain terms: companies whose core business is being eaten by generative AI. Not "AI will help them" — "AI will replace what they do." Examples in this bucket: VisasQ (expert-consulting platform competing with AI search), HEROZ (legacy AI software leapfrogged by LLMs), NSD (person-month SI where AI compresses the engineer-hour cost they bill). Rule used: ticker-specific override for names where Round 2 verification documented AI as a structural threat to the business model. Why this matters: the derating is not a temporary glitch — it is the market re-pricing the long-term TAM (total addressable market) of these businesses. These stocks can stay cheap for years unless the company successfully pivots to "AI-augmented" services or finds a defensible niche (proprietary data moats, regulated industry barriers, etc.). What to watch: explicit AI-strategy pivots from management, partnerships with AI infrastructure players (OpenAI, Anthropic, hyperscalers), and the competitive response (price cuts, service redesign, new product launches).
4382 HEROZ, Inc. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -50.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4382 + minkabu 4382 + logmi finance HEROZ + irbank 4382
What happened: HEROZ became famous for the shogi AI 'Ponanza' (an AI that beat professional shogi players), but the current core business is B2B AI services — 'AIX' (developing and providing enterprise AI agents and AI solutions), and 'AI Security' (using AI for cyber defense). FY4/2024 was excellent: revenue +62.4% to ¥4.84 billion, operating profit +75% to ¥451 million. FY4/2026 Q3 continued the trend: revenue +7.8% to ¥4.69 billion, OP +126.4% to ¥358 million — sharp improvement. Q3 cumulative net profit is still -¥49 million (a loss, but narrower than before). The full-year guide is revenue ¥6.7 billion (+13.0%) and operating profit ¥800 million (+161.1%) — strong growth on top of last year. The recurring-revenue share (the portion of revenue that's stable and recurring) is over 60%, making the business model relatively stable. Net profit stays thin because operating profit gets reinvested into new products like 'HEROZ ASK.' Why: AI-agent feature implementations and large-deal wins are growing the AIX segment. AI Security is also growing. Both businesses ride the AI boom tailwind. But continued front-loaded investment in new-product development holds net profit down. Stock reaction: Results are improving, but several structural problems stack up on the stock. The most striking indicator: HEROZ's PBR (price-to-book ratio — how many times the company's book value the stock costs) is 0.68x as of 2025. That's the 'below-1.0x' state — meaning the stock is trading BELOW the company's break-up value. In other words, the market thinks 'the future value of this company's business is less than its current net assets.' Unusual for an AI-related name. Second, the net-margin problem. Operating profit is positive, but net margin remains negative. The past 12 quarters show improvement, but profitability is still in recovery — stability is limited. Investors still doubt 'is this actually a money-making business?' Third, relative valuation against AI peers. Many AI-related names trade at high PERs (sometimes over 100x), but HEROZ trades below book value. That means the market is signaling 'given HEROZ's profitability and capital efficiency, PBR-below-1 is fair valuation.' Profit stability is the bottleneck. Fourth, business-model and growth uncertainty. Investment in new products like 'HEROZ ASK' (an enterprise AI assistant) compresses profit, making short-term profit improvement difficult. The market isn't yet convinced the investment will pay off. Fifth, sector-wide derating. The Information & Communications sector broadly has a hard time getting credit relative to the attention it gets. Even AI peers without clear monetization paths face market caution. Sixth, small/mid-cap problem. HEROZ is a TSE Standard mid-cap, with limited major-broker coverage. Structural absence of buyers. 1-year total return -2.92%; 52-week range ¥758-¥1,374 — long-term the stock isn't moving much.

4490 VisasQ Inc. internet_platform small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -55.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4490 + Nikkei article on Coleman impairment + note HR793 article on Coleman + minkabu 4490 + logmi finance 4490
What happened: VisasQ runs a 'spot consulting' marketplace — basically a platform where companies can pay subject-matter experts for one-hour advisory calls on specific topics. FY2/2026 Q3 cumulative: operating revenue was slightly down, but operating, ordinary, and net profit all rose — net profit jumped a remarkable +156.5%. BUT the full-year guide projects revenue UP / profit DOWN, with operating profit at ¥1.03 billion (-16.1%). Q2 was weak: operating revenue -3.3% to ¥4.77 billion, OP -14.2% to ¥499 million. Why: The domestic business is holding up well; the overseas business (Coleman — discussed below) is the real problem. Q3's net-profit jump was thanks to short-term cost discipline, not a fundamental fix. Stock reaction: The single biggest reason the stock has been stuck long-term is this: in 2021, VisasQ acquired the US-based Coleman Research Group for about ¥10 billion. At the time it was framed as 'a stepping stone for global expansion.' But after the deal, the US M&A market cooled and Coleman's earnings deteriorated sharply. The result: VisasQ booked a ¥14.4 billion impairment loss (where the goodwill was deemed unrecoverable) plus a ¥14.5 billion special loss. Equity (the shareholders' stake) was damaged nearly to the point of insolvency. That wound is still being carried, and investor trust takes time to rebuild. Several structural problems also stack on top. VisasQ has medium-term targets — like ¥30 billion GMV by FY2/2030 — that management plans to hit by 'deliberately sacrificing near-term profit to prioritize growth investment.' That creates a classic 'growth-stock trap': revenue keeps rising but EPS (earnings per share) stagnates, which makes the stock hard to value upward. There's also a market-size problem. VisasQ is the dominant leader in Japan's spot-consulting market, but the market itself has a ceiling that's getting close (this is what investors call a 'TAM limit' — Total Addressable Market). Plus, spot consulting is a labor-intensive model (you're matching humans to humans for one-off engagements), so margins can't expand the way they do in pure-software SaaS. Supply-and-demand on the stock is also unfavorable — a lot of investors bought at past highs and are still holding at a loss. Every time the stock tries to rally, those 'bag holders' sell to break even (Japanese investors call this 'yare-yare uri' or 'phew sell'), which caps every bounce. Finally, there are concerns about preferred-share dividend obligations. All of these factors combine to keep VisasQ's stock from ever fully re-rating.

9759 NSD CO.,LTD. SI_IT_services mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: +2.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 9759 + Yahoo finance 9759 forum (specific investor view quoted) + Nikkei 9759 + 株予報Pro 9759
What happened: NSD is a Japanese independent IT company — they provide system development, maintenance, and operations services to corporate clients. They cover a wide range: DX (digital transformation) system development, financial IT (bank and insurance core systems), industrial IT (manufacturing systems), and security products. FY3/2026 results were excellent: revenue +9.3% to ¥117.81 billion, operating profit +13.2% to ¥19.07 billion, ordinary profit +13.4% to ¥19.33 billion. The DAS segment (Digital Architecture Solution — cloud-based DX system development), the priority focus area, grew +15.8% to ¥57.58 billion and led overall growth. Why: Large-enterprise DX needs are expanding, and NSD's strengths in financial IT and industrial IT are both growing. Security products are also doing well. Long-term net and OP margins trend upward; ROE (return on equity) and ROA (return on assets) are above conventional thresholds — stable profitability. These are the metrics of 'a solid IT company.' Stock reaction: As of May 12, 2026, the stock is at ¥2,625 versus a consensus target of ¥4,200 (60% upside on paper) — but the valuation just won't catch up. The biggest structural concern shows up in candid investor remarks on message boards and articles: 'NSD is not a SaaS company. It's a per-task / staffing maintenance & operations business, easily AI-cost-compressed; the bounce is weak. Compare it to NRI and TIS, which are monetizing AI.' Translated, this is concern about the business model itself in the AI era. Specifically: First, the nature of NSD's business model. NSD is NOT 'a high-growth SaaS (recurring-revenue cloud service)' — it's a labor-intensive IT company running 'contract development + staffing-style engagements (dispatching engineers to client sites) + maintenance & operations.' This kind of business carries high risk of AI substitution or cost compression — when a client realizes 'this maintenance work can be done by AI,' NSD's deal size or volume gets squeezed. Second, NSD looks worse in peer comparison. Within the same Japanese IT industry, NRI (Nomura Research Institute) and TIS (TIS Inc.) are launching AI-powered new services and converting in-house products to SaaS — they're 'monetizing AI.' When investors compare NSD to them, NSD looks like it's 'falling behind in the AI era.' Third, the valuation problem. NSD's forward PER (price-to-earnings ratio) is 21.7x, and PBR (price-to-book ratio) is 3.86x. Versus theoretical fair value, this is already 'fairly valued' — the good results are already priced in. For further upside, 'significant earnings improvement' is needed, but the current business model doesn't lend itself to explosive growth. Fourth, broader system-integration industry direction. Big competitors are pivoting toward SaaS, AI-related services, and consulting — all higher-value-added areas. NSD's revenue mix still leans heavily on traditional contract development and maintenance, leaving it relatively weaker in market perception. Fifth, the market's demand. Investors want to see 'will future earnings push theoretical fair value higher' — steady growth alone produces 'the stock looks cheap, but I can't buy it (because the upside isn't visible)' indefinitely. Until NSD shows a concrete roadmap for transforming its business in the AI era, the stock is likely to stay parked at this undervalued level.

Mature giant drifting — too slow for growth money, not safe enough for defensive money 3 names
In plain terms: big established companies that grew revenue but the stock barely moved — or drifted lower. They are too slow for growth-seeking capital (revenue +3-5% is uninspiring) but they may not be defensive enough — earnings still swing with the consumer / ad / tech cycle. Examples in this bucket: MIXI, Kakaku.com, LY Corporation. Rule used: ticker-specific override for names whose stock-business gap reflects being "stuck in the middle" of investor style boxes. Why this matters: these are often "good but not great" investments. They generate cash, pay dividends, and rarely make headlines — but capital allocators have nowhere to put them in their style buckets, so they drift. A meaningful catalyst (buyback acceleration, special dividend, business split, M&A, activist arrival) can move them out of this zone. What to watch: TSE PBR-improvement disclosures, capital allocation policy changes, and any signal that the company is reframing itself for one specific investor style (yield / value / growth).
2121 MIXI,Inc. internet_platform mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -10.6%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 2121 + Nikkei article on MIXI profit upward revision + gamebiz 2121 + Note 運営型モバイルゲーム article
What happened: MIXI is originally known for the huge mobile-game hit 'Monster Strike' (Monst), but the company has been diversifying — it now operates three pillars: a Digital Entertainment segment (centered on Monst), a Sports segment (TIPSTAR online keirin betting + the Chiba Jets B-League basketball team), and a Lifestyle segment. FY3/2026: revenue +10.7% to ¥171.4 billion (up), but operating profit -16.3% to ¥22.3 billion (down). Most recent Q1: revenue +4.1% to ¥31.3 billion, with Sports and Lifestyle leading. Why: Sports (TIPSTAR + Chiba Jets) grew strongly, but Digital Entertainment's main pillar 'Monster Strike' saw monthly active users (MAU) decline — that segment's revenue fell -13.1% to ¥21.9 billion. A one-time bump in TV-anime ad spend also pushed EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) down -14.8% to ¥10.4 billion. Monst's per-user ARPU (average revenue per user) is up, but retaining new users is the issue. Management aims to revive MAU via a UI/UX refresh and AI-guide features. Stock reaction: The stock is up +13.89% over the past 12 months — a slow but real medium-term uptrend. The year-high was ¥2,795 (Jan 13), the year-low was ¥2,449 (Mar 30), and it's trading around ¥2,710 lately. Not a bad outcome — but several structural factors cap further upside. First, the Monst problem. The game launched in 2013 — over 10 years ago. Long operation has made the controls complex and harder for new players to adopt. Management is guiding Monst revenue down next year too, signaling continued gaming-business decline. More critically — as Toyo Keizai notes, about 70% of MIXI's total sales depend on Monst — this single-title concentration risk is widely recognized as MIXI's biggest strategic challenge. On the upside, TIPSTAR (online keirin betting) saw MAU growth of +80% YoY in the Oct-Dec quarter, and Chiba Jets is benefiting from last year's opening of LaLa arena TOKYO-BAY through ticket and sponsorship growth. Second, the mobile-game industry as a whole has structural problems. Japan's mobile-game market has been flat for several years now. Chinese games like Genshin Impact and Azur Lane are now standard in Japan, which means from a Japanese company's perspective, the domestic share keeps shrinking. There's also fierce competition for users' time from video-streaming platforms (YouTube, TikTok, etc.). Third, sports-betting regulatory risk. TIPSTAR is a reliable growth engine, but as a gambling-related business it carries social-perception issues, and conservative institutions tend to avoid it. Mobile-game 'gacha' (loot-box) mechanics are also psychologically classified as gambling-addiction-inducing, and have a history of regulatory tightening (banning comp-gacha, requiring probability disclosure, etc.). Fourth, structural cap from institutional investors. The whole gaming sector is seen as 'high-gambling-character,' which makes conservative institutions stay away. Smartphone-game companies are also viewed as having 'a ceiling on growth potential,' which makes their valuation multiples hard to expand. Re-rating triggers — (a) Monst's ARPU sustainability getting confirmed for another year, (b) Sports business continuing double-digit growth, or (c) announcement of a new game title or new business.

2371 Kakaku.com,Inc. internet_platform mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -4.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 2371 + Nikkei 2371 + 株予報Pro 2371
What happened: Kakaku.com is one of Japan's largest comparison-and-review website operators. It runs multiple services including Kakaku.com (price comparison for electronics and household goods), Tabelog (restaurant reviews), Job Box (job search engine), and others. FY3/2026: revenue +20.0% to ¥94.13 billion (strong growth), but operating profit -7.0% to ¥27.24 billion (decline). Parent-attributable net profit -6.1%. Why: Tabelog grew well and drove revenue, but the Job Box business posted a loss that squeezed consolidated operating profit. Management's choice to front-load growth investment is showing up — revenue is rising, but profit isn't being captured yet. FY3/2027 guidance is for revenue +21.6% and OP +13.1%, signaling the company is targeting double-digit growth medium-term. Stock reaction: The 'mixed signal' (revenue up, profit down) has split the market view, and there's no big move in the stock post-earnings. A few structural reasons make it hard for the valuation to expand. First, margin compression. Both net and operating margins fell YoY, and EPS (earnings per share) growth slowed. Investors are wary that revenue growth isn't converting to profit growth. Second, the valuation question. Kakaku.com's forward PER (price-to-earnings ratio — how many years of future earnings the stock costs) is about 27.96x. That sits in the middle of its historical range (14.95-63.98x), meaning growth expectations are already priced in. For further upside, results need to outperform what's already expected. Third, the business-portfolio complexity. Kakaku.com has four segments — Kakaku.com price-comparison, Tabelog, Job Box, and Incubation (new-business development). Each moves differently, which makes the valuation model complex for institutions and makes ongoing coverage harder. Fourth, the structural problem with Job Box. In the job-search-engine world, Indeed (operated by Recruit) is an overwhelming global giant. It's not clear how Job Box differentiates against that. On the other hand, Kakaku.com's medium-term plan through FY3/2030 positions Job Box as the most important growth area, targeting ¥50 billion in revenue (5-year CAGR +31%) — so the current losses are deliberate front-loaded investment. Investors are trying to gauge 'when does this loss turn into profit.' Fifth, AI threat to Tabelog. Tabelog is already mature. And longer-term, AI-based recommendation services (think ChatGPT recommending restaurants) could emerge and fundamentally change how users discover places to eat, putting Tabelog's moat at risk. Re-rating triggers — (a) Job Box turning profitable, (b) Tabelog re-accelerating on inbound-tourism demand, or (c) new AI-related services.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):

4689 LY Corporation internet_platform large
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -0.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4689 + LY Corp Q1 決算短信 + Nikkei Veritas search ad share AI article + KO投資ブログ LINEヤフー analysis + Toyo Keizai プライム脱落 article
What happened: LY Corporation (LINE Yahoo Corporation) was formed in 2021 by the merger of Yahoo Japan and LINE — they operate many of Japan's central internet services: the search engine 'Yahoo! JAPAN,' the messaging app 'LINE,' e-commerce ('Yahoo! Shopping,' 'ZOZOTOWN'), and payments ('PayPay'), among others. FY3/2026 results are strong: revenue ¥2,036.3 billion (+6.2%), adjusted EBITDA ¥496.6 billion (+5.5%) — both record highs. Net profit +26.2% to ¥193.6 billion — a 3-year record. BUT pretax profit of ¥294.2 billion (+7%) was 10.4% short of the IFIS consensus (analyst average) of ¥328.5 billion. Why: Yahoo-LINE post-merger monetization is going OK — ads, commerce, and PayPay-integration synergies are gradually converting to profit. But full-year growth is slowing: ad-market stagnation, weak domestic macro, and concerns about the pace of synergy materialization persist. Stock reaction: Even with these record-high profits, the stock is structurally stuck. Why? Several serious problems compound. First, ad-revenue stagnation. In the Media business, share-decline concern + the rise of AI search services pressing on ad revenue are spreading anxiety. Search-ad revenue at LINE-Yahoo properties (their own sites) AND partner sites both fell YoY. This is a structural threat — AI search like ChatGPT and Perplexity is rising, and users may stop using traditional search results (where ads live). Second, chronically low EPS (earnings per share). In the 2021 LINE merger, 2.8 billion new shares were issued (on top of the existing 4.8 billion), instantly inflating share count by about 60%. Even when profits arrive, the per-share dilution drags EPS. Third, the information-leak issue and loss of security trust. Past LINE info-leak incidents left lingering market distrust of governance. Fourth, supply-demand worsening from new-share warrants. The large issuance of warrants in 2021-2022 also hit, and the stock fell from over ¥800 in November 2021 to the ¥300s by late 2022. Fifth, ROE (return on equity) of just 5.1% — low. ROE measures management capital efficiency, and 5% counts as weak. It gives investors evidence that 'this company isn't using shareholder money efficiently.' Sixth, tough competition. Like Google and Meta, growth is slowing for LY. Yahoo's ad-revenue share is about 60%, so it's heavily exposed to ad-market deceleration. Direct competition with Google, Meta, and Microsoft is fierce. The target price consensus is ¥549, leaving a 39.43% gap from current price (real upside on paper). But without resolving the structural problems, that gap won't close.

Special situation — one-off corporate, commodity, or market-structure event 5 names
In plain terms: the company grew revenue but the stock fell because of a specific one-time event — not a broad pattern. Examples in this bucket: TerraSky (a quantum-computing JV the market is still digesting), Remixpoint (crypto/energy price volatility wagging the tail), coconala (market-microstructure liquidity collapse), Secure (company-specific operational issues), Trend Micro (MBO speculation reversal). Rule used: ticker-specific override for names with documented one-off drivers. Why this matters: these are NOT pattern trades — they are event trades. Each name needs an individual deep dive into the specific event, its expected resolution timeline, and whether the impact is reversible. Do NOT treat them as a group thesis. What to watch: the event-specific resolution timeline per name (JV milestones for TerraSky, commodity prices for Remixpoint, MBO process steps for Trend Micro, daily liquidity tape for coconala).
3825 Remixpoint, Inc. other small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -21.4%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3825 + minkabu 3825 + EnergyShift article on power-retail margins + 投資の達人 3825 article
What happened: Remixpoint is a unique company combining several different businesses — electricity retailing, energy-storage solutions, and (recently growing fast) a 'Digital Asset Management' business that holds and operates cryptoassets, especially as a Bitcoin treasury. FY3/2026 Q1 saw a dramatic rebound — revenue +50.8% to ¥6.49 billion, and operating profit +3,137% (more than 31x the prior year!) to ¥1.74 billion. Ordinary profit jumped 43x YoY to ¥1.76 billion; operating margin surged from 1.2% (prior year) to 26.8%. Why: The Q1 rebound came mainly from launching a Bitcoin Treasury business and booking valuation gains on held cryptoassets — basically 'paper gains' from rising Bitcoin prices, not from core-business improvement. The equity ratio (the portion of assets not financed by debt) rose to 91.9%, so the balance sheet is very strong. Management is going harder on Bitcoin: a 25th issuance of subscription warrants and 4th unsecured bonds will raise about ¥31.5 billion, most of which is earmarked for buying more Bitcoin. Stock reaction: Q1 was great, but several structural factors keep the stock from getting long-term credit. First, no guidance. Remixpoint hasn't disclosed FY3/2026 consolidated guidance. The reason: 'it's difficult to rationally estimate the crypto-holdings size and the impact on consolidated results.' FY3/2027 guidance is also not disclosed. The 'non-disclosure' itself spooks institutions — when management can't commit to numbers, investors can't model future earnings. Second, no analyst coverage. Major brokerage analysts don't cover Remixpoint, so institutional buyers stay away. Third, structural weakness in the core business (electricity retailing). The retail-electricity market in Japan is small (about ¥0.1 trillion nationwide) and structurally low-margin — it's a fragile industry. So Remixpoint's 'core' isn't a confidence-inspiring pillar. Fourth, crypto dependency and regulatory risk. The cryptoassets are stored with external custodians. Any cyberattack, system failure, or regulatory tightening could meaningfully threaten asset preservation. Fifth, supply-demand manipulation flagged. Investor message boards and articles point out a pattern: 'institutions buy low 2-4 weeks before earnings, then sell into the post-earnings retail buying interest.' This means retail investors often end up buying at the top. The core businesses aren't bad — but cryptoasset unrealized losses (when prices fall) keep dominating every quarterly print. The market treats Remixpoint as 'essentially a leveraged Bitcoin play / Bitcoin proxy.' When Bitcoin rises, the stock recovers; when Bitcoin falls, the stock falls harder.

3915 TerraSky Co., Ltd. other small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -7.3%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 3915 + Yahoo finance 3915 + Quantum-computing JV announcement news
What happened: TerraSky is the leading Japanese partner in the Salesforce (the world's biggest customer-relationship-management SaaS) ecosystem — TerraSky helps companies implement Salesforce, customize it, and run it as a managed service. Earnings (announced Jan 14, 2026) revenue +12.2% to ¥20.3 billion — double-digit growth held. Operating profit was -7.5% because of upfront investment in their in-house product business. Net profit jumped +89.4% thanks to a special gain (a one-time item). The most recent full-year results (FY2/2026): ordinary profit ¥1.72 billion (+7.7% YoY) BEAT the ¥1.48 billion guide. The FY2/2027 guide is for ordinary profit +52.2% to ¥2.62 billion — which would be 3 consecutive years of record profit. Why: TerraSky's leadership in the Salesforce ecosystem is its durable growth thesis. As more Japanese companies adopt Salesforce, TerraSky's work expands. Product-business investment temporarily compressed OP, but beating the guide proved management's execution. Stock reaction: The stock recently entered a sharp surge phase — over the prior 12 months it had been -11.01%, but last week +18.17%, past month +46.04% — a violent reversal. What's the catalyst for the recent surge? A wave of quantum-computing joint-research announcements from TerraSky's group company 'Quemix.' On June 1, 2026, Quemix disclosed multiple partnerships at once: Quemix × Mitsui Kinzoku (a new technology called QAVG for material calculations on quantum computers), Quemix × Nissan Motor (next-gen aerodynamic analysis software using a quantum-classical hybrid algorithm — patent filed), Quemix × Denso (foundation technology for running molecular dynamics simulations on quantum computers), and Quemix × Toyota Motor + Toyota Central R&D Labs + the University of Tokyo (efficient compute-resource allocation between classical and quantum hardware). Then on June 3, Quemix × Honda Technical Research was added — exponential acceleration of density-functional calculations on quantum computers. The combined 'quantum + Japan's biggest automakers and materials companies' (Nissan + Mitsui Kinzoku + Denso + Toyota + Honda) story laid a fresh growth layer on top of the Salesforce thesis, re-rating the stock. In other words, TerraSky is the classic 'overlooked name suddenly gets attention from a new news event' breakout pattern. The stock that wouldn't move suddenly got the 'flashy theme' (quantum computing) it needed to attract investor attention. A few structural cautions remain. First, the margin issue — revenue +12.2% but OP -7.5%. Profitability hasn't caught up to revenue growth. If product-business investment continues, margin recovery will take time. Second, valuation. Forward PER is now 26.0x and PBR is 3.19x — relatively high. Market expectations are already priced in, so future results need to back them up. Third, scale. With a market cap of ¥35.4 billion (mid-cap), major-institutional coverage is still limited. Sustained re-rating requires bigger institutions to start buying. Fourth, Salesforce-strategy risk. TerraSky's business depends heavily on Salesforce. If Salesforce changes its Japan strategy (e.g., shifts toward more direct sales), TerraSky's work could shrink. If the FY2/2027 +52.2% OP guidance is delivered, further re-rating is likely.

4176 coconala Inc. internet_platform small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -12.5%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4176 + Nikkei 4176 + minkabu 4176 + SaaS sector death article
What happened: coconala is a 'skills marketplace' — a C2C platform where individuals sell their skills (design, fortune telling, legal consultation, writing, business consulting, etc.) to other individuals and businesses. They also run an Agent service for corporate clients. FY5/2025 was dramatic: revenue +167.0% YoY, net profit +124.9%. Most recent FY8/2025: revenue ¥9.41 billion, net profit ¥306 million — continued growth. Revenue expanding YoY, EPS rising, and free cash flow (cash left over after capex) improving vs prior year. Why: On the Marketplace side, the legal-consultation segment hit a record — more people buying single-shot consultations with lawyers and other professionals, lifting the share of higher-priced specialized categories and raising overall profitability. The Agent business completed restructuring and V-shape recovered. AI-related categories (AI-prompt writing, AI-tool consulting, etc.) saw demand surge, while at the same time AI-resistant categories (handmade goods, in-person services) also grew — coconala is benefiting from BOTH sides of the AI boom. Stock reaction: Despite the clean fundamentals, the stock is up just +0.51% over the past 12 months — essentially flat. And longer term, from the listing-high of ¥2,899 to the listing-low of ¥219, it's down -92%. Market participants candidly say: 'Business is growing but the stock doesn't budge — doesn't look like a growth stock.' That's the long-standing puzzle. Several structural factors combine. First, the whole growth-stock sector is derating. In recession-leaning environments, defensive sectors (healthcare, consumer staples, telecom — things demand doesn't change in a downturn) get favored. With the Growth market broadly weak and Information & Communications valuations slumping, coconala gets dragged along. Second, small-cap status. coconala's market cap is only ¥9.5-9.7 billion — labeled 'Analyst-coverage: none.' Institutional research and position-building are limited. Buyers are structurally missing. Third, liquidity. Trading volume is thin, so larger institutions can't build positions easily — buying enough to matter would push the stock up by itself. Fourth, business-model concerns. Market participants flag two issues: 'AI driving low-price gigs to grow' (AI like ChatGPT can do simple tasks, pushing prices down — a platform-wide threat) and 'seller-quality decline' (new sellers' quality is falling, hurting transaction experience). Strong numbers don't translate to PER recognition because these structural concerns make investors anticipate future profit erosion. Fifth, sector-rotation pressure. Investor sentiment is shifting from 'growth → value,' so growth-leaning small-caps are getting derated broadly. Re-rating triggers — (a) continued record-breaking results, (b) graduation to the TSE Prime market, or (c) stronger IR effort toward institutional investors.

4264 Secure, Inc. SaaS small
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -19.8%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4264 + minkabu 4264 + fisco 4264
What happened: Secure is a physical-security company with two main products. The first is 'SECURE AC' — access-control systems for offices and factories (controlling entry/exit using IC cards and biometrics). The second is 'SECURE VS' — surveillance cameras combined with video analytics (using AI to analyze movement and faces). FY12/2026 Q1 was strong: revenue +31.0% to ¥2.37 billion, operating profit +54.0% to ¥234 million. Why: Both main products are winning mid/large-deal contracts — shifting from small-deal-focused to bigger-ticket business. Specifically, data centers, factories, and offices are increasing their adoption. Stock reaction: Q1 numbers were good, but several structural concerns make it hard for the valuation to settle. First, the longer-term trend. Looking across 12 trailing quarters (3 years), the business has been somewhat weak. Net margin and ROE (return on equity) are down YoY, signaling profitability momentum is dulling. Q1 alone looked good, but the longer trend isn't reassuring. Second, balance-sheet dynamics. The equity ratio (the portion of assets not financed by debt) has recovered, but at the same time interest-bearing debt (debt requiring repayment) has risen, and EPS (earnings per share) has been unsteady. The balance-sheet picture is mixed. Third, margin issues. Operating and net margins are down YoY, with only limited improvement momentum recently. Q1's strong print came from 'large-deal wins' as a positive driver, but it isn't enough to reverse the overall margin trend. Fourth, ROE/ROA (return on equity / return on assets) sit below the conventional 8-10% threshold considered healthy. On capital-efficiency criteria, Secure doesn't clear institutional bars. Fifth, competition. The access-control and surveillance markets are crowded — in Japan, ALSOK (Sohgo Security) and SECOM dominate the enterprise tier; overseas players like Axis Communications and Hikvision (China) are also pushing in. How Secure differentiates within this is an ongoing challenge. Sixth, the small-cap problem. As a TSE Growth small-cap, institutional coverage is limited — buyers are structurally missing. For Q1's print to drive a real re-rating, Secure needs (a) several quarters of sustained margin improvement, (b) balance-sheet stabilization (debt reduction), and (c) clear differentiation in AI video analytics.

4704 Trend Micro Incorporated SaaS mid
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: -24.1%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 4704 + minkabu 4704 + moomoo 4704 earnings
What happened: Trend Micro is a Japan-headquartered global cybersecurity major — they sell antivirus, endpoint (PC/mobile) security, and cloud security products worldwide for both consumer and enterprise markets. About 70% of revenue comes from overseas, which is unusually international for a Japanese company. FY12/2025 saw revenue and profit growth; AI-powered next-gen SOC (Security Operations Center) products led growth in particular. Cost discipline also worked, pushing operating profit +20.1%, and the equity ratio improved. Trailing 12 quarters (3 years) show operating and net margins consistently improving YoY. Why: Cybersecurity demand is structurally expanding — as companies migrate to the cloud and remote work, more attack surface needs defending. AI-era threats (generative-AI phishing, deepfake fraud) are also rising, driving enterprise security spending. Trend Micro's new 'AI-SOC' (AI-powered security operations center) products are contributing meaningfully. Stock reaction: The FY12/2025 print was strong, but FY12/2026 expects revenue to grow while operating profit slightly declines due to cost increases — that's capping upside. An important framing — Trend Micro is unusual in this list of 41 R+×S- names. It's a large-cap (market cap in hundreds of billions of yen) with deep institutional ownership and deep major-broker analyst coverage. It's a 'normally functioning stock' — there are plenty of rational market participants reacting to fundamentals. Most other names in this set have problems (no analyst coverage, tiny cap, no liquidity) that distort price discovery; Trend Micro doesn't. The stock's forward path follows three factors. First, cyber-attack frequency in the news. Every time a major cyberattack hits headlines, demand for Trend Micro's products rises and pricing power increases. When attack news is quiet, customers defer product upgrades. Second, yen weakness. Since ~70% of revenue is overseas, a weak yen (= strong dollar) boosts results substantially when translated back to yen. FX is a major earnings driver. Third, AI-driven differentiation. The US has formidable competitors — CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Palo Alto Networks. How well Trend Micro can integrate AI into its own products and stay differentiated will drive long-term valuation. For an investor wanting both value (cheap) and quality (good), Trend Micro is the cleanest (most understandable) name in this list.

Sustained compounder 1 names
In plain terms: this company has put up "all-three-up" results (revenue, operating profit, net profit all up) for 3 or more years in a row, AND the stock has tracked that compounding growth each year. Rule used: 3 or more consecutive Q1 years (Q1 = all-three-up + stock-up). Why this matters: the market has clearly recognized the quality of durable growth, and rewards it consistently. These names typically trade at premium multiples (high PER), but the multiple is justified by the consistency. The downside is concentrated in moments where the streak might break — disappointment after long success can trigger sharp corrections. What to watch: any signal that the multi-year growth streak is slowing (single-quarter miss, guidance cut, key customer loss). As long as the streak continues, the simple decision is to hold and watch the compounding.
9697 CAPCOM CO.,LTD. content_games large
Revenue: up Stock 2025 YoY: +5.1%
Genuine research (JP web search per company) 📚 kabutan 9697 + 株予報Pro 9697 + IFIS 株予報 9697 + Yahoo finance 9697 + QUICK Money World 大和 upgrade article + timebank shoken 9697 article + Capcom integrated report 2025 + diamond.jp game-stock analyst panel + single-pass combined-prompt verification 2026-06: kabutan 9697 + Nikkei 9697 + president.jp Capcom strategy article
What happened: Capcom is a Japanese game company holding many globally famous IPs (intellectual property): Monster Hunter, Resident Evil (バイオハザード), Street Fighter, Devil May Cry, and more. They operate across console games, PC games, mobile, arcade, merchandise, esports, and film adaptations. Q3 cumulative results were excellent — revenue ¥115.3 billion (+29.8%), operating profit ¥54.3 billion (+75.1%) — strong top and bottom growth. The flagship Digital Contents business (game software sales) and the Amusement business (arcade equipment and venue operation) both recovered, contributing to the result. Why: Capcom's biggest strength is the 'catalog sales' model — past hit titles (Monster Hunter, Resident Evil, etc.) keep selling via digital download for years after release, with revenue spikes tied to new-title launches. This stretches the revenue peak of each new title and creates a structurally stable profit base. They also distribute across multiple platforms (Nintendo Switch, PS5, Steam, etc.), so they're diversified by geography and platform too. Stock reaction: As of the May 13, 2026 full-year results, the stock was at ¥3,150 with forward PER 22.7x and PBR 4.93x. Forward ROE 21.7% and forward ROA 17.09% show very high profitability; market cap is ¥1.65 trillion. The market values Capcom's durable growth story highly. Several structural reasons explain why business and stock are rising together. First, overwhelming IP strength. The Monster Hunter series is consistently a blockbuster in Japan, and each new release also boosts sales of prior entries. Resident Evil retains massive global popularity, and IP-licensing revenue from films, TV series, and theme parks is also expanding. Second, the quality of profitability. ROE above 21% is exceptional for a game company — it means stable catalog sales combined with new launches produce structurally high margins. Investors deeply value 'high margin quality.' Third, 12-quarter improvement trend. Performance has been consistently improving, with revenue expansion and stable profitability coexisting. That makes Capcom an 'easy-to-forecast name' — ideal for institutional portfolios. Fourth, investor psychology. Investors cite 'fundamentals 67% / technicals 33%' as drivers of upside expectations — meaning the rally is recognized as fundamentals-driven, which is the healthiest kind. Fifth, AI-theme tailwind. Capcom is researching AI applications in game development, adding another 'AI exposure' angle. This is a textbook case of fundamentals-and-stock virtuous cycle. Institutional long-term holding demand is heavy. [Single-pass combined-prompt verification (2026-06)] Revenue ¥195.3 billion (+15.2%), OP ¥75.2 billion (+14.5%), NP ¥54.5 billion (+12.7%) — 11 consecutive years of profit growth. Digital sales ratio ~83%, OP margin 38-39%. Repeat-title share: 50% (2015) → over 70% (2023). Overseas revenue ~80%, 230+ countries. OP 5x over 10 years. Monster Hunter Wilds: 8 million units in 3 days. Jeffries maintains Buy. Market expects 10% growth, company tracking 24% — PER ~30x. Caveat: Wilds sales below expectations triggered drop from ¥4,800 ATH to ¥4,000 range.
Pattern (bucket-templated label):
4-year sustained-grower template — business and stock both up each year. 2025 stock +5.1% YoY. Market is following the fundamentals.

Sign summary: revenue rose +11.2%; OP rose +15.3% (record); NP rose +11.9% (record); 5 consecutive years of record OP+NP; close +5.1% YoY. 業績 ◎ (5-year record streak) / 株価 ◎ (close +5.1%, with Jul 1 peak ¥5,015).
